EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long wave trough was over the eastern U.S. with a swath of moisture confined over central Florida and points southward. Scattered severe storms developed during the afternoon and evening along the sea breeze fronts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of heavy rain that left the ground saturated, strong winds developed over northeast Georgia behind a strong cold front. After a period of heavy rain that left the ground saturated, strong winds developed over northeast Georgia behind a strong cold front. The combination of very windy conditions and wet ground resulted in numerous fallen trees, which brought down power lines and damaged homes and cars.

A light mix of sleet and snow developed over the mountains and foothills of northeast Georgia during the evening, and continued for much of the overnight. Accumulations ranged from trace amounts over the lower elevations of the foothills, to localized 2-3 inch amounts over the mountains.  A few slick spots developed on mountain roads, resulting in quite a few accidents.
A few severe thunderstorms moved across the CSRA taking down trees and powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak back door cold front pushed through the region, resulting in erratic winds causing a prescribed burn to develop into a wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During a prescribed burn the fire spotted over a road, so a head fire was set against that thinking it would contain it.  The fire blew up when the main fire met with the head fire, causing the fire to get hot and spot over another road onto private land.  The total acreage burned by this entire fire was 130.80 acres.  The number of acres burned outside of the prescribed burn area totaled 45.80 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter/firefighter for Lumpkin county reported that more than 100 trees were blown down in the Mill Creek Station Subdivision in western Lumpkin county near the Dawson county border, west of Georgia Highway 9.  One house suffered significant damage from a downed tree and a vehicle was smashed from a fallen tree.  Many of the trees were uprooted or twisted off approximately 20 feet above ground level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced nickel size hail in the upper portions of the CSRA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported nickel size hail in the norther portion of the county near Phinizy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved across the CSRA and produced large tornadoes.  One tornado tracked across Columbia and Richmond counties then went into Aiken county South Carolina along highway 278.  The second tornado tracked across Burke county then into lower Barnwell and Allendale counties in South Carolina. Several homes were destroyed and many had moderate to severe damage.  Widespread trees and powerlines were also down.  Total damage estimate was 3 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An supercell tornado tracked across Burke county and severely damaged several homes and buildings.  Numerous trees and powerlines were damaged.  There was one critical injury and several other minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough was developing across the eastern U.S. as a large polar high dropped south out of central Canada and a vigorous closed upper low moved from the central U.S. into the southeast.  Warm, moist, unstable air in advance of this system supported the development of widespread showers and thunderstorms.  Marginal severe hail and thunderstorm wind events were observed, but by far the main concern was flooding.  Several creeks and streams exceeded bankfull from this heavy rain event.  The flooding continued into the first few days of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harris County 911 Center reported that a woman was struck by lightning and injured.  No further information was provided.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattahoochee County Emergency Management Director reported that at least three trees were down southwest of Cusseta along Riverbend Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter-sized hail approximately three miles south of Dalton on Old Dixie Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough was developing across the eastern U.S. as a large polar high dropped south out of central Canada and a vigorous closed upper low moved from the central U.S. into the southeast.  Warm, moist, unstable air in advance of this system supported the development of widespread showers and thunderstorms.  Marginal severe hail and thunderstorm wind events were observed, but by far the main concern was flooding.  Several creeks and streams exceeded bankfull from this heavy rain event.  The flooding continued into the first few days of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Schley County Regional 911 Center reported that a few trees were down on the north side of Buena Vista near the power station and on Dr. Deryl Hart Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that a brief EF1 tornado touched down approximately two miles northwest of Sparta and traveled only 1.5 miles before lifting.  This was the first of three tornadoes to touch down in Hancock county between 1019 pm EDT and 1028 pm EDT, each near Sparta.  This tornado was determined to have a maximum path width of 100 feet with maximum winds estimated at 80 mph.  This first tornado tracked through a quite rural area.  However, one mobile home suffered moderate damage to its roof, siding, and windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low was sweeping through the Great Lakes into the northeast U.S., with a deep trough extending southward toward the Gulf Coast.  A strong polar cold front accompanied the system.  Unseasonably cold air was spreading from the northern plains toward the southeast behind the front as a 1036 millibar surface high moved south from Canada.  A very unstable air mass was noted ahead of the front across the southeast U.S.  However, a complex of thunderstorms which moved east out of central Alabama across central Georgia during the morning hours, greatly stabilized the atmosphere across the area.  Consequently, little severe weather was observed despite the strength of the weather systems tracking across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pike County Journal and Reporter of Zebulon reported that lightning struck a group of 16 cows lying down near a tree at a dairy farm on Kings Road near Meansville.  All of the cows were dead at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface front extended across central Georgia and the big bend region of North Florida and an upper level trough approached from the west. These mechanisms fueled scattered severe storms across southeast Georgia in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Oak Bend Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail in the northwest part of the county west of Resaca and in Resaca, half-dollar-sized hail just north of Calhoun, and penny-sized hail in and just east of Calhoun.  This was the first of two severe thunderstorms to track across Gordon county during the evening of April 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite moist ground conditions from abundant recent rainfall, a human-caused wildfire was observed buring 4.2 acres in the Shoal Creek area of Jones county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported a human-caused wildfire in the Shoal Creek area of Jones county.  The fire consumed 4.2 acres of forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A drier spell during the end of April combined with gusty winds and low relative humidity resulted in two additional wildfires in the northeast Georgia mountain county of Union. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported a human-caused wildfire.  The fire was labeled Sharp Top and consumed 3.5 acres.

While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators and the public observed penny-sized hail from Peachtree City to Brooks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed half-dollar-sized hail in the Chickamauga area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that the EF1 tornado that initially touched down in extreme eastern Glascock county continued into far northern Jefferson county along a five mile-long path before lifting approximately three miles northeast of Stapleton.  The tornado was determined to have a maximum path width of 200 yards with maximum winds estimated at 110 mph.  The majority of the damage associated with this tornado occurred in Jefferson county.  Significant damage was observed to the Heartsgrove Baptist Church at the intersection of Georgia Highways 80 and 296, just across from the Glascock county line.  Part of the roof, brick siding, and the chimney were removed from the structure.  Several granite headstones in the adjacent cemetery were tipped over.  Many trees were down along the path of the tornado, especially along a 3/4 mile-long path east of the damaged church.

One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed a funnel cloud near Ellaville.  Doppler radar data clearly indicated a strong mesoscale circulation passing just south of Ellaville at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across central Georgia and produced 1 to 2 inches of snow in northern Lincoln county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow fell in extreme northern Lincoln county in the Beulah and Graball areas as a winter storm moved across northern Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The sea breezes initiated storms across a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail near Patterson on Highway 32.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The sea breezes initiated storms across a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail occurred near Hoboken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The sea breezes initiated storms across a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down and minor roof damage occurred to barns on the north side of Hoboken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mean layer ridge stretched across central Florida. A cold front extended across the southeast region from the Carolinas to the eastern Gulf coast states. Storms developed across the area in the afternoon as the front approached from the north in an unstable and moist airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EMA director reported golfball size hail and strong winds in northeast Appling county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface front extended across central Georgia and the big bend region of North Florida and an upper level trough approached from the west. These mechanisms fueled scattered severe storms across southeast Georgia in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported on County Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough was developing across the eastern U.S. as a large polar high dropped south out of central Canada and a vigorous closed upper low moved from the central U.S. into the southeast.  Warm, moist, unstable air in advance of this system supported the development of widespread showers and thunderstorms.  Marginal severe hail and thunderstorm wind events were observed, but by far the main concern was flooding.  Several creeks and streams exceeded bankfull from this heavy rain event.  The flooding continued into the first few days of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River gages on Brooks, Beach, and Harris Creek indicated that flooding occurred.  All three exceeded bankfull during the indicated time frame.  Flooding along Brooks Creek caused Francis White Road, about seven miles east-northeast of Buchanan, to be closed for several hours.  Flooding, which occurred at the intersection of Beach and Harris Creek approximately six miles west-southwest of Buchanan, caused Beach Creek Road to be flooded and closed for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak and broad upper trough was progressing slowly from the mid-south into the southeast U.S. from March 15th through the 16th.  A weak cold front accompanied the system and was located across southern Alabama and southern Georgia, with a surface low pressure system located along the front across southeastern Alabama.  Although instability was non-existent because of a relatively cool, stable air mass over the region, strong low-level shear and a low level jet were evident across central Georgia.  The shear was sufficient enough that a heavy shower, with no indicated cloud-to-ground lightning, produced a brief 2-mile long EF0 tornado across eastern Marion and western Schley counties during the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marion County Emergency Management Director reported that the tornado, which began in eastern Marion county approximately two miles northwest of Putnam, continued on a two-mile east-northeastward track into extreme western Schley county.  The tornado lifted approximately 2.1 miles northwest of Walls Crossing.  The tornado continued to have a maximum path width in Schley county of 100 yards.  Within Schley county, nearly 500 trees were either snapped or uprooted along the brief 1/2 mile long path.  No structural damage was reported within Schley county.

Radar data, and reports from neighboring counties to the west and east, supports that hail this size or larger likely fell in a west to east swath across the county from west of Dawonsville along the Pickens county line to the Hall county line east of Dawsonville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia, confirmed that an EF1 tornado had touched down just west of a home at the end of Bay Circle off an inlet of Lake Lanier.  The tornado tracked across this home and across a small inlet of Lake Lanier to another home at the end of Bay Court on the other side of the lake inlet.  At least 50 large trees were uprooted or snapped around the home off Bay Circle, several of which fell on and caused considerable damage to the structure.  Two vehicles in the driveway of this home were crushed when large trees fell on them.  Three boats and two boat docks were destroyed.  The boat docks, and one of the boats, were blown inland some 200 yards, overturned, and portions wrapped around or underneath fallen trees.  The home on Bay Court on the other side of the Lake Lanier inlet suffered minor roof and structural damage from downed trees.  However, at least another 50 large trees were uprooted or snapped on the adjacent land between the home and the lake inlet.  The tornado lifted as it approached a steep hill behind the home on Bay Court.  A couple of adjacent homes also suffered minor roof and window damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dodge County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down across a utility pole causing a power outage along Chester Highway in northeast Dodge county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Data gathered from the Wilcox County Emergency Management Director and from a damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia concluded that the EF1 tornado that began in the Cobb community of far southeast Sumter county and tracked east-southeast across Crisp county, continued into far southwest and south central Wilcox county before lifting.  The total path length of the tornado was around 33 miles.  Damage was sporadic along the path of the tornado.  The tornado tracked approximately 12 miles within Wilcox county and remained as a weaker EF0 tornado with a maximum path width of 100 yards and maximum winds of 80 mph.  Within Wilcox county, most of the damage was concentrated in the Owensboro area, with just isolated tree damage from Hatley, along the Crisp/Wilcox county line to the west side of Ownesboro.  One home at the intersection of County Roads 60 and 61, just southwest of Owensboro, suffered considerable damage.  A mobile home was destroyed just east of the intersection of Georgia Highways 233 and 238.  Finally, considerable damage was noted to a pecan orchard east of County Road 63 or about one mile east-southeast of Owensboro, near the point where the tornado lifted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported a human-caused wildfire.  The fire was labeled Cooper Gap and consumed 3.5 acres.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed nickel-sized hail in the Eatonton area.  Radar data suggests that penny to nickel-sized hail likely fell across much of the central portion of the county from west to east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail in the far northern part of the county in the Hamburg State Park area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southern stream short wave was moving from the southern plains toward the southeast.  A cold front remained nearly stationary across the Tennessee Valley and into the Southern Plains.  A long-lived squall line of severe thunderstorms tracked from the mid-south during the morning hours into the southeast by late afternoon, reaching the Georgia border around 5 pm.  The line of thunderstorms weakened quickly as it progressed further eastward into the state, but not before blowing down several hundred trees and power lines, especially in parts of west central Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was slowly moving from the central U.S. toward the eastern U.S.  An accompanying cold front and Canadian high pressure system were moving southeast from the central U.S.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during afternoon heating in an increasing moist and unstable atmosphere. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow was once again well established across Georgia thanks to a large subtropical ridge anchored across the Texas/Oklahoma region.  A disturbance in the northwest flow aloft across the Ohio Valley and an associated cold front helped to support convection across the region during the afternoon and evening hours.  A few storms in north Georgia approached severe limits and produced 1/4 to 1/2 inch-diameter hail and 40 to 45 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dade County Emergency Management Director reported that a couple of trees and one power line were down in the northern part of the county between Sand Mountain and Trenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north Florida and Southeast with a series of disturbances riding eastward along the boundary. Gulf moisture fed the storms with heavy Rainfall occurring across most of the region. On Thursday a strong disturbance over the Rockies became a low pressure system that finally drove a cold Front through the area on Thursday night. Jet dynamics were favorable for strong to severe storms during the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dam break due to heavy rainfall occurred at a spillway from Lake Hillard in Coffee County. Twenty families were evacuated. The width of the dam break was about 75 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unusually mid-summer broad upper trough was located across the central U.S., with moist and active southwest flow aloft across the southeastern U.S.  A slow moving cold front was approaching the area from the Tennessee Valley.  Waves of isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms continued, even during the nighttime hours, during the last several days of July and into the first few days of August.  One early morning thunderstorm in Carroll county in west central Georgia spawned a lightning-related fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Times Georgian of Carrollton reported that lightning struck a home on Bear Creek Road during the early morning hours as the family inside was sleeping.  The lightning strike caused a fire in the attic, resulting in minor to moderate damage to the structure.  No injuries were reported, however.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy Rainfall over a two week period, but more significantly extremely heavy rainfall over a 36 hour period pushed Forks Creek into flood. Tree debris left over from clear cutting of local tree farms was damming against the bridge and the Ware County Public Works Department had to use two cranes to move the debris from the upstream side of the Forks Road Bridge to the downstream side to prevent  potential damage to the bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS crossed over southeast Georgia during the morning. The remnant upper level short wave trough crossed over northeast Florida during the afternoon which destabilized the atmosphere significantly to yield numerous severe storms in the afternoon and evening. The modified KJAX 12Z sounding indicated surface based lifted indices of -8 and CAPES of 4200 J/kg were possible during maximum heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a barn fire in Jamestown. The barn was completely destroyed, but the barn was not in use and ownership of the property was unknown. Another lightning strike also caused extensive damage to the Ware County Sheriff's Department radio equipment, cameras, computers and gate openers. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering surface low pressure center was over south-central Georgia and surface high pressure was positioned well south of the area. These ingredients combined to yield a severe storm that produced a weak tornado in Glynn county in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed over the Little Satilla River and tracked quickly northeast impacting the Royal Oaks Subdivision. Damage occurred to a Sunoco Gas Station near the intersection of Interstate 95 and U.S. Highway 17 which included a collapsed wall and damaged windows. Numerous trees were blown down with minor damaged noted to some residences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southern stream short wave was moving from the southern plains toward the southeast.  A cold front remained nearly stationary across the Tennessee Valley and into the Southern Plains.  A long-lived squall line of severe thunderstorms tracked from the mid-south during the morning hours into the southeast by late afternoon, reaching the Georgia border around 5 pm.  The line of thunderstorms weakened quickly as it progressed further eastward into the state, but not before blowing down several hundred trees and power lines, especially in parts of west central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that three trees were blown down within the city of Athens.  No structural damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little had changed with the upper atmospheric pattern across the southeast U.S. for several days.  A mean negatively tilted trough continued across the region, while a stationary front persisted near the Georgia-Tennessee border southwest into the mid-south.  Warm, moist, unstable air remained in place across Georgia, especially middle Georgia.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms persisted across central Georgia throughout the day.  A few of these briefly became severe and produced marginal severe hail in the eastern parts of middle Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail around Kite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms over the Gulf of Mexico produced outflows during the morning that converged with the east coast sea breeze during the early afternoon. A moist and unstable airmass was in place, as well as a lingering surface trough across south Georgia. Widespread showers and storms developed. An upper level COL region was over the area which provided very slow storm motion. Doppler radar rainfall estimates generally ranged from 3-5 inches where nearly stationary and back-building cells developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported golfball size hail in Dixie Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance drove a squall line or Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) through southeast Georgia in the early morning hours.  Another MCS pushed into the region and collided with the sea breeze in the evening, and resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across the area once again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported several trees down across Highway 23 along the Jenkins and Emanuel county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The broad upper low continued to drift very slowly east-northeast from east Texas during this period.  Showers and thunderstorms became more numerous and very efficient rain producers across west and north Georgia.  Tropical moisture continued to spread northward into the region.  Precipitable water values increased to over two inches.  The repeated rainfall across the same areas primed the stage for the record, historical, and catastrophic flood event that unfolded from the 21st into the 22nd.  On the 19th, the bulk of the flood and flash flooding was confined to the western and northern suburbs of Atlanta, especially early on the 19th, when a good 6-hour period of heavy showers and thunderstorms trained across south Fulton, Cobb, Cherokee, Forsyth, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties, dumping five to seven inches of rain in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clayton county Emergency Management personnel reported that general flooding continued across a small portion of far northern Clayton county as late morning and early afternoon flash flooding subsided.  Additional minor property damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across the northern Gulf of Mexico.  A fast westerly flow aloft was present across the region with a broad upper trough evident across the mid-south region.  An unusually warm, moist air mass was present across central and south Georgia.  The upper trough combined with the unstable, moist atmosphere to result in thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening.  One of the storms became severe and produced severe hail and an isolated tornado in the central Georgia county of Sumter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County Emergency Management Director confirmed that an EF1 tornado touched down and tracked a little over two miles across the southern part of the county.  The tornado touched down approximately five miles south of Americus near U.S. Highway 19.  A grocery store on U.S. Highway 19, just south of McLittle Bridge Road, suffered a collapsed wall and roof damage.  A large tree also fell on a forestry building near the location.  A nearby home also suffered minor damage.  The tornado continued to a point approximately 5.5 miles southeast of Americus or near Georgia Highway 377.  Approximately 50 to 100 trees were uprooted along the path of the tornado.  Some power lines were also down along the path of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another flood event plauged north Georgia, just two weeks after the catastrohpic and historical flooding of late September.  However, this event was considerably less severe than the September event.  A stationary front across the northern Gulf of Mexico and a large Canadian high across the northeast United States combined with abundant pacfic moisture and a strong southwesterly jet aloft, set the stage for a significant overrunning rain event across north Georgia.  Several counties across north Georgia experienced minor to moderate flooding.  Many streams, creeks, and rivers across this region were in flood once again for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on Noses Creek near Powder Springs reached its flood stage of 11.0 feet at 742 pm EDT and remained in flood until 1130 pm EDT.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal adjacent to the creek.

A stationary front across the northern Gulf of Mexico and a large Canadian high across the northeast United States combined with abundant pacfic moisture and a strong southwesterly jet aloft, set the stage for a significant overrunning rain event across north Georgia.  Several counties across north Georgia experienced minor to moderate flooding.  Many streams, creeks, and rivers across this region were in flood once again for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on the Little River near Woodstock reached its flood stage of 11 feet at 333 pm EDT.  The Etowah River near East Canton reached its flood stage of 17 feet at 856 pm EDT.  Flooding subsided after 0130 am EDT on the 13th.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal adjacent to the rivers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad cutoff upper low, which had been located over central Texas, was beginning to move very slowly east by the 16th.  This upper low, in combination with unusually deep tropical moisture across the region for mid-September, began an extensive period of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain.  The deep tropical moisture was largely the result of a persistent 850 mb flow from the Gulf of Mexico.  The pattern changed very little for nearly a week as the upper low drifted very slowly east, completely cutoff from the main belt of westerlies, which were located well to the north in southern Canada.  The first flash flood event of an extended period of historical and record flooding across north and central Georgia, began on the 15th in Gilmer county.  A few storms became strong to marginally severe as well.||A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event began to unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during this time, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily across parts of north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Morgan County Emergency Management Director reported that considerable flooding continued across the county much of the night following earlier flash flooding.  Several roads, especially in the southern part of the county, remained under water and closed, particularly U.S. Highway 441 and Georgia Highway 83.  Several creeks and streams in the central and southern part of the county were out of their banks.  Minor additional road damage was observed during this period.

The first flash flood event of an extended period of historical and record flooding across north and central Georgia, began on the 15th in Gilmer county.  A few storms became strong to marginally severe as well.||A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event began to unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during this time, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily across parts of north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail in the Holly Springs area as well as southeast of Waleska.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down just off highway 123 near the South Carolina state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida, which formed in the Caribbean and intensified to a category two hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson scale, moved north from the Caribbean across the central Gulf of Mexico and then inland along the U.S. Gulf coast near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th.  The remnants of the hurricane then moved east-northeast across southern Alabama and southern Georgia before moving off the east coast of the U.S. as a strong surface low pressure area.  Heavy rainfall, on the order of four to five inches, was common across north Georgia, with one to two inch amounts across central areas.  With the ground totally saturated from several prior months of heavy rainfall, widespread creek, stream, and river flooding was observed over a two to three day period.  Because the rain intensity was mostly moderate or less and extended over a period of 18-24 hours, flash flooding events were isolated, with most of the flood events being of the creek, stream, and river flood nature.||The small wind core of Hurricane Ida and its track across land significantly reduced the wind effects of this system on north and central Georgia.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 20 mph range with a few stronger gusts.  Only Banks county in northeast Georgia observed any wind damage, where a few trees and power lines were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida moved inland near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th and then tracked east-northeast across southern Alabama, southern Georgia, and the Florida Panhandle before emerging off the east coast as a strong low pressure system.  The remnants of the hurricane combined with a cold air wedge across north Georgia to bring abundant Atlantic and Gulf moisture into the region.  Twelve to 18-hour rainfall totals of 4-5 inches across north Georgia aggrevated totally saturated soils from an on of the wettest September and October periods on record to result in widespread creek, stream, and river flooding.  The generaly light to moderate intensity of the rainfall and its persistence over a long period of time resulted in limited flash flooding.  Damages from Hurricane Ida to Georgia were confined to minor flooding, mostly adjacent to rivers, creeks, and streams.  Some locations experienced flooding subsequently for several days.  Only 15 to 20 mph winds with some gusts to 25 mph were observed with the remnants of Ida as the tightly concentric wind field weakened rapidly with the inland movement.  In addition, no tornadoes were observed with the remnants of Ida as it tracked across the region.  Total damages from Ida within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were well less than $100,000.

Because the rain intensity was mostly moderate or less and extended over a period of 18-24 hours, flash flooding events were isolated, with most of the flood events being of the creek, stream, and river flood nature.||The small wind core of Hurricane Ida and its track across land significantly reduced the wind effects of this system on north and central Georgia.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 20 mph range with a few stronger gusts.  Only Banks county in northeast Georgia observed any wind damage, where a few trees and power lines were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida moved inland near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th and then tracked east-northeast across southern Alabama, southern Georgia, and the Florida Panhandle before emerging off the east coast as a strong low pressure system.  The remnants of the hurricane combined with a cold air wedge across north Georgia to bring abundant Atlantic and Gulf moisture into the region.  Twelve to 18-hour rainfall totals of 4-5 inches across north Georgia aggrevated totally saturated soils from an on of the wettest September and October periods on record to result in widespread creek, stream, and river flooding.  The generaly light to moderate intensity of the rainfall and its persistence over a long period of time resulted in limited flash flooding.  Damages from Hurricane Ida to Georgia were confined to minor flooding, mostly adjacent to rivers, creeks, and streams.  Some locations experienced flooding subsequently for several days.  Only 15 to 20 mph winds with some gusts to 25 mph were observed with the remnants of Ida as the tightly concentric wind field weakened rapidly with the inland movement.  In addition, no tornadoes were observed with the remnants of Ida as it tracked across the region.  Total damages from Ida within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were well less than $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad cutoff upper low, which had been located over central Texas, was beginning to move very slowly east by the 16th.  This upper low, in combination with unusually deep tropical moisture across the region for mid-September, began an extensive period of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain.  The deep tropical moisture was largely the result of a persistent 850 mb flow from the Gulf of Mexico.  The pattern changed very little for nearly a week as the upper low drifted very slowly east, completely cutoff from the main belt of westerlies, which were located well to the north in southern Canada.  The first flash flood event of an extended period of historical and record flooding across north and central Georgia, began on the 15th in Gilmer county.  A few storms became strong to marginally severe as well.||A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event began to unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during this time, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily across parts of north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the upper portion of Peachtree Creek was observed during the evening of September 16th from persistent heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  The lower portion of Peachtree Creek, where the USGS gage is located, did not flood during this event.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal in areas adjacent to the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended across the area from just inland of St. Simons Island southwestward to just north of the Florida-Georgia state line near Valdosta. A surface low was broadening and weakening as it approached the Florida panhandle. Just enough surface based instability and moderate dynamics created an isolated severe thunderstorm threat across the area during the afternoon. There was a marginal tornado threat near the warm front and an isolated severe thunderstorm threat south of the front across the Florida peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees which landed on power lines. Power had to be restored to areas along and near Highway 82 and State Road 110 across eastern Brantley and western Glynn counties by the Okefenokee Rural Electric Membership Corporation. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad cutoff upper low, which had been located over central Texas, was beginning to move very slowly east by the 16th.  This upper low, in combination with unusually deep tropical moisture across the region for mid-September, began an extensive period of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain.  The deep tropical moisture was largely the result of a persistent 850 mb flow from the Gulf of Mexico.  The pattern changed very little for nearly a week as the upper low drifted very slowly east, completely cutoff from the main belt of westerlies, which were located well to the north in southern Canada.  The first flash flood event of an extended period of historical and record flooding across north and central Georgia, began on the 15th in Gilmer county.  A few storms became strong to marginally severe as well.||A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event began to unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during this time, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily across parts of north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County 911 Center reported flash flooding from Turtle and Sandy Run Creeks approximately four miles southwest of Warner Robins.  Several homes along Feagin Mill Road experienced flooding as a result.  Water was at least a foot deep in one of the affected homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A predawn severe thunderstorm on the 16th produced a few reports of wind damage in Lowndes and Lanier counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on a vehicle on Clover Drive.  Another large tree blocked traffic on East Alden Avenue.

In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during this time, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily across parts of north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bibb County Emergency Management Director reported that flooding continued across much of the county following earlier flash flooding.  Several roads remained under water and closed.  Additional minor damage to roads and property was observed during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad cutoff upper low, which had been located over central Texas, was beginning to move very slowly east by the 16th.  This upper low, in combination with unusually deep tropical moisture across the region for mid-September, began an extensive period of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain.  The deep tropical moisture was largely the result of a persistent 850 mb flow from the Gulf of Mexico.  The pattern changed very little for nearly a week as the upper low drifted very slowly east, completely cutoff from the main belt of westerlies, which were located well to the north in southern Canada.  The first flash flood event of an extended period of historical and record flooding across north and central Georgia, began on the 15th in Gilmer county.  A few storms became strong to marginally severe as well.||A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event began to unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that three trees were down approximately four miles west of Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of rain fell across parts of extreme northeast Georgia. Several creeks came out of their banks in Elbert County as a result of the prolonged period of heavy, stratiform rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After 3 to 4 inches of rain fell over a widespread area in about a twelve-hour period, flooding developed along Wahachee Creek, affecting portions of Edison Rd and Dr. George Ward Rd. Both roads were closed. Also, Beaverdam Creek overflowed its banks in the northern part of the county, flooding Horse Farm Rd and Brickyard Rd and flooding a restaurant on Brewers Bridge Rd. Kurt Anderson Rd was also closed for a while.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The broad upper low continued to drift very slowly east-northeast from east Texas during this period.  Showers and thunderstorms became more numerous and very efficient rain producers across west and north Georgia.  Tropical moisture continued to spread northward into the region.  Precipitable water values increased to over two inches.  The repeated rainfall across the same areas primed the stage for the record, historical, and catastrophic flood event that unfolded from the 21st into the 22nd.  On the 19th, the bulk of the flood and flash flooding was confined to the western and northern suburbs of Atlanta, especially early on the 19th, when a good 6-hour period of heavy showers and thunderstorms trained across south Fulton, Cobb, Cherokee, Forsyth, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties, dumping five to seven inches of rain in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on the South River at Hapeville in central Fulton county reached its flood stage of 15 feet at 135 pm EDT.  The river remained above flood stage at this location until 536 pm EDT.  One home on Macon Drive was flooded by the river.  A car on Macon Drive also because submerged in water and was carried downstream.  Flooding was also reported along Camp Creek near Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport where the parking lots of several apartments were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that lightning struck a home in the 2700 block of Maple Vista Lane.  The resulting fire caused minor damage to the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida, which formed in the Caribbean and intensified to a category two hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson scale, moved north from the Caribbean across the central Gulf of Mexico and then inland along the U.S. Gulf coast near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th.  The remnants of the hurricane then moved east-northeast across southern Alabama and southern Georgia before moving off the east coast of the U.S. as a strong surface low pressure area.  Heavy rainfall, on the order of four to five inches, was common across north Georgia, with one to two inch amounts across central areas.  With the ground totally saturated from several prior months of heavy rainfall, widespread creek, stream, and river flooding was observed over a two to three day period.  Because the rain intensity was mostly moderate or less and extended over a period of 18-24 hours, flash flooding events were isolated, with most of the flood events being of the creek, stream, and river flood nature.||The small wind core of Hurricane Ida and its track across land significantly reduced the wind effects of this system on north and central Georgia.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 20 mph range with a few stronger gusts.  Only Banks county in northeast Georgia observed any wind damage, where a few trees and power lines were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida moved inland near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th and then tracked east-northeast across southern Alabama, southern Georgia, and the Florida Panhandle before emerging off the east coast as a strong low pressure system.  The remnants of the hurricane combined with a cold air wedge across north Georgia to bring abundant Atlantic and Gulf moisture into the region.  Twelve to 18-hour rainfall totals of 4-5 inches across north Georgia aggrevated totally saturated soils from an on of the wettest September and October periods on record to result in widespread creek, stream, and river flooding.  The generaly light to moderate intensity of the rainfall and its persistence over a long period of time resulted in limited flash flooding.  Damages from Hurricane Ida to Georgia were confined to minor flooding, mostly adjacent to rivers, creeks, and streams.  Some locations experienced flooding subsequently for several days.  Only 15 to 20 mph winds with some gusts to 25 mph were observed with the remnants of Ida as the tightly concentric wind field weakened rapidly with the inland movement.  In addition, no tornadoes were observed with the remnants of Ida as it tracked across the region.  Total damages from Ida within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were well less than $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front shifted north through portions of southeast Georgia during the late evening and early night hours. As this occurred, a series of 500 millibar shortwaves shifted through the southeast. Precipitable water values around 1.5 inches in combination with shortwave and frontal forcing led to several west to east training thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported that parts of Pope Road near Highway 119 were flooded and impassable. Adjacent land areas were also extensively flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the Southeast in the morning which then allowed strong warm air advection to occur during the late morning and early afternoon hours. A cold front then swept through southeast Georgia early in the afternoon. A few strong storms developed behind a the cold front where strong low level winds and sufficient moisture remained before shifting over the Atlantic Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a funnel cloud in Bloomingdale, Georgia.

Twelve to 18-hour rainfall totals of 4-5 inches across north Georgia aggrevated totally saturated soils from an on of the wettest September and October periods on record to result in widespread creek, stream, and river flooding.  The generaly light to moderate intensity of the rainfall and its persistence over a long period of time resulted in limited flash flooding.  Damages from Hurricane Ida to Georgia were confined to minor flooding, mostly adjacent to rivers, creeks, and streams.  Some locations experienced flooding subsequently for several days.  Only 15 to 20 mph winds with some gusts to 25 mph were observed with the remnants of Ida as the tightly concentric wind field weakened rapidly with the inland movement.  In addition, no tornadoes were observed with the remnants of Ida as it tracked across the region.  Total damages from Ida within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were well less than $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida, which formed in the Caribbean and intensified to a category two hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson scale, moved north from the Caribbean across the central Gulf of Mexico and then inland along the U.S. Gulf coast near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th.  The remnants of the hurricane then moved east-northeast across southern Alabama and southern Georgia before moving off the east coast of the U.S. as a strong surface low pressure area.  Heavy rainfall, on the order of four to five inches, was common across north Georgia, with one to two inch amounts across central areas.  With the ground totally saturated from several prior months of heavy rainfall, widespread creek, stream, and river flooding was observed over a two to three day period.  Because the rain intensity was mostly moderate or less and extended over a period of 18-24 hours, flash flooding events were isolated, with most of the flood events being of the creek, stream, and river flood nature.||The small wind core of Hurricane Ida and its track across land significantly reduced the wind effects of this system on north and central Georgia.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 20 mph range with a few stronger gusts.  Only Banks county in northeast Georgia observed any wind damage, where a few trees and power lines were blown down.

The generaly light to moderate intensity of the rainfall and its persistence over a long period of time resulted in limited flash flooding.  Damages from Hurricane Ida to Georgia were confined to minor flooding, mostly adjacent to rivers, creeks, and streams.  Some locations experienced flooding subsequently for several days.  Only 15 to 20 mph winds with some gusts to 25 mph were observed with the remnants of Ida as the tightly concentric wind field weakened rapidly with the inland movement.  In addition, no tornadoes were observed with the remnants of Ida as it tracked across the region.  Total damages from Ida within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were well less than $100,000.

Twelve to 18-hour rainfall totals of 4-5 inches across north Georgia aggrevated totally saturated soils from an on of the wettest September and October periods on record to result in widespread creek, stream, and river flooding.  The generaly light to moderate intensity of the rainfall and its persistence over a long period of time resulted in limited flash flooding.  Damages from Hurricane Ida to Georgia were confined to minor flooding, mostly adjacent to rivers, creeks, and streams.  Some locations experienced flooding subsequently for several days.  Only 15 to 20 mph winds with some gusts to 25 mph were observed with the remnants of Ida as the tightly concentric wind field weakened rapidly with the inland movement.  In addition, no tornadoes were observed with the remnants of Ida as it tracked across the region.  Total damages from Ida within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were well less than $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida, which formed in the Caribbean and intensified to a category two hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson scale, moved north from the Caribbean across the central Gulf of Mexico and then inland along the U.S. Gulf coast near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th.  The remnants of the hurricane then moved east-northeast across southern Alabama and southern Georgia before moving off the east coast of the U.S. as a strong surface low pressure area.  Heavy rainfall, on the order of four to five inches, was common across north Georgia, with one to two inch amounts across central areas.  With the ground totally saturated from several prior months of heavy rainfall, widespread creek, stream, and river flooding was observed over a two to three day period.  Because the rain intensity was mostly moderate or less and extended over a period of 18-24 hours, flash flooding events were isolated, with most of the flood events being of the creek, stream, and river flood nature.||The small wind core of Hurricane Ida and its track across land significantly reduced the wind effects of this system on north and central Georgia.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 20 mph range with a few stronger gusts.  Only Banks county in northeast Georgia observed any wind damage, where a few trees and power lines were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Herald Gazette of Barnesville reported that Potato Creek on the west side of the county experienced minor flooding.  A 10-acre pond on U.S. Highway 41 in Redbone failed and flooded adjacent areas.  Only adjacent agricultural areas were affected.  Damages were mostly a result of the dam failure and associated debris removal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Butts County Emergency Management Director reported that portions of Lucy Lane and Lunsford Road were covered with water much of the day and closed.  The flooding was a result of high water from the South River at Snapping Shoals.  The river crested at 12.9 feet with flood stage at 12.0 feet.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal from the affected roads.

With the ground totally saturated from several prior months of heavy rainfall, widespread creek, stream, and river flooding was observed over a two to three day period.  Because the rain intensity was mostly moderate or less and extended over a period of 18-24 hours, flash flooding events were isolated, with most of the flood events being of the creek, stream, and river flood nature.||The small wind core of Hurricane Ida and its track across land significantly reduced the wind effects of this system on north and central Georgia.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 20 mph range with a few stronger gusts.  Only Banks county in northeast Georgia observed any wind damage, where a few trees and power lines were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida moved inland near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th and then tracked east-northeast across southern Alabama, southern Georgia, and the Florida Panhandle before emerging off the east coast as a strong low pressure system.  The remnants of the hurricane combined with a cold air wedge across north Georgia to bring abundant Atlantic and Gulf moisture into the region.  Twelve to 18-hour rainfall totals of 4-5 inches across north Georgia aggrevated totally saturated soils from an on of the wettest September and October periods on record to result in widespread creek, stream, and river flooding.  The generaly light to moderate intensity of the rainfall and its persistence over a long period of time resulted in limited flash flooding.  Damages from Hurricane Ida to Georgia were confined to minor flooding, mostly adjacent to rivers, creeks, and streams.  Some locations experienced flooding subsequently for several days.  Only 15 to 20 mph winds with some gusts to 25 mph were observed with the remnants of Ida as the tightly concentric wind field weakened rapidly with the inland movement.  In addition, no tornadoes were observed with the remnants of Ida as it tracked across the region.  Total damages from Ida within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were well less than $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another flood event plauged north Georgia, just two weeks after the catastrohpic and historical flooding of late September.  However, this event was considerably less severe than the September event.  A stationary front across the northern Gulf of Mexico and a large Canadian high across the northeast United States combined with abundant pacfic moisture and a strong southwesterly jet aloft, set the stage for a significant overrunning rain event across north Georgia.  Several counties across north Georgia experienced minor to moderate flooding.  Many streams, creeks, and rivers across this region were in flood once again for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several USGS stream gages indicated flooding on creeks and streams across Gwinnett county.  At 914 am EDT, Pew Creek near Lawrenceville reached its flood stage of 11.0 feet.  At 1020 am EDT, Level Creek near Suwanee reached its flood stage of 9.0 feet.  At 1138 am EDT, the Alcovy River near Lawrenceville reached its flood stage of 9.0 feet.  At 1150 am EDT, Crooked Creek near Norcross reached its flood stage of 11.0 feet.  At 1259 pm EDT, Big Haynes Creek reached its flood stage of 10.0 feet.  Finally, the Yellow River near Lithonia reached its flood stage of 13.0 feet at 135 pm EDT.  Flooding continued along the creeks, streams, and rivers through 230 pm EDT.  However, damage was confined to minor debris removal adjacent to the creeks and streams.

The generaly light to moderate intensity of the rainfall and its persistence over a long period of time resulted in limited flash flooding.  Damages from Hurricane Ida to Georgia were confined to minor flooding, mostly adjacent to rivers, creeks, and streams.  Some locations experienced flooding subsequently for several days.  Only 15 to 20 mph winds with some gusts to 25 mph were observed with the remnants of Ida as the tightly concentric wind field weakened rapidly with the inland movement.  In addition, no tornadoes were observed with the remnants of Ida as it tracked across the region.  Total damages from Ida within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were well less than $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low developed over the northern Gulf of Mexico Wednesday morning and intensified as it shifted northeast over Georgia. As this process occurred, deep moisture advected over the Southeast within the warm sector of the low pressure system. Strong isentropic ascent ahead of the surface low followed by a cold front in the evening helped produce several strong thunderstorms through early Wednesday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported one tree down along the intersection of Loves Chapel Road and Peggy Garrison Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving area of low pressure moved across the southern Appalachians during the afternoon of December 2nd. Widespread 3 to 4 inch rainfall totals were reported with the system. As the ground was already quite moist from several recent rain events, a few areas of flash flooding developed. A small area of thunderstorms developed on the southern end of the rain area. The storms produced some isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along highway 79 about 12 miles east southeast of Elberton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong southern stream short waves were embedded within a large scale mean trough located across the central into the northeast United States.  Moist Pacific and Gulf flow was evident across the southern and southeastern states in advance of these disturbances.  This led to multiple rounds of showers and a thunderstorms during the period of the 13th through the 15th until a strong Arctic cold front swept through the entire eastern two-thirds of the U.S., pushing the showers and thunderstorms well south of the area.  The heavy rain on totally saturated ground from recent months of much above normal rainfall led to renewed flooding and flash flooding, especially across central Georgia, where two to four inches of rain was common during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Treutlen County Emergency Management Director reported minor flooding of some roads and property across the county, mostly adjacent to overflowing creeks and streams.  Monetary damage was confined to minor debris removal in these areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County Emergency Management Director reported that several ponds were over flowing their dams from heavy rain across the county.  Three dams in particular were affected, including McMath Mill Pond Dam, Murphy Mill Dam, and Lake Collins.  These ponds were 110 to 120 acres large.  These were as a result of excessive rain runoff from Wolf Creek, Mill Creek, and Parkers Mill Creek, respectively.  Fortunately, none of the dams actually failed.  Two people were rescued from their vehicle from flood waters from Mill Creek on Murphy Mill Road.  Several dirt roads across the county were washed out, but none of the paved roads suffered damage.  No injuries were reported.  Monetary damage was confined to debris removal in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid and upper low initially over Texas was moving east-northeast while the upper level trough it was embedded in became negatively tilted. This led to a broad diffluent pattern over most of the southeastern states. As the system approached the right rear quadrant of upper jet moved into the forecast area coincident with a low-level jet of, at least, 60 knots. This created atmospheric dynamics favorable for severe weather, with the only limiting factor being modest instability. A warm front over the area during the morning moved rapidly north in response to increasing southerly flow allowing a gradual increase in low level instability. By afternoon several severe weather events and three tornadoes occurred over portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down at approximately 1415EST in northwest Pierce County near Merson Road and Crump Road. Several trees were damaged and a farm shed had portions of its sheet metal roof removed. Winds here were estimated to have been near 70 mph or EF-0. The tornado moved northeast across several fields and encountered two small farm structures and a wooded area along Tiny Lane. The structures were completely destroyed with their contents strewn across the wooded area. Extensive tree blow down occurred in the woods with many pines snapped at 10 to 15 feet above ground level.  The tornado was estimated to have been an EF-1 event in this location with winds of 110 mph.  The funnel continued to move across fields next encountering wooded areas and structures near Scuffletown Road. At this point the funnel was one quarter mile or approximately 440 yards wide with winds estimated at 105 mph. Extensive tree blow down continued here with one mobile home destroyed and substantial damage to a wood frame structure.  The tornado crossed the Big Satilla Creek at this point, and crossed into Appling County, continuing on a northeast track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another very wet weather system was moving across Georgia.  An upper trough in the central U.S. was shifting slowly east.  A surface low pressure system was moving east-northeast along the eastern Gulf Coast and across north Florida.  Ample moisture and dynamics combined to bring another round of moderate to heavy rain to north and central Georgia.  Widespread rainfall amounts of two to four inches were observed across north Georgia, with one to two inches across central Georgia.  The rain fell over a 24 to 36 hour period.  The ground remained totally saturated from previous heavy rain events in recent weeks and an extraordinarily wet fall.  While no flash flooding was observed because of the medium intensity and long duration of the rainfall event, general flooding was observed in the west central Georgia county of Haralson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haralson County Emergency Management Director reported flooding from local creeks in the county.  Beech Creek was flooding portions of Old Ridgeway Road, five miles northwest of Bremen, and Brooks Creek was flooding portions of Frances-White Road, seven miles northeast of Buchanan.  Monetary damage was confined to minor debris removal adjacent to the creeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of heavy rain that left the ground saturated, strong winds developed over northeast Georgia behind a strong cold front. The combination of very windy conditions and wet ground resulted in numerous fallen trees, which brought down power lines and damaged homes and cars.

Strong mid and upper low initially over Texas was moving east-northeast while the upper level trough it was embedded in became negatively tilted. This led to a broad diffluent pattern over most of the southeastern states. As the system approached the right rear quadrant of upper jet moved into the forecast area coincident with a low-level jet of, at least, 60 knots. This created atmospheric dynamics favorable for severe weather, with the only limiting factor being modest instability. A warm front over the area during the morning moved rapidly north in response to increasing southerly flow allowing a gradual increase in low level instability. By afternoon several severe weather events and three tornadoes occurred over portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down at approximately 1415EST in northwest Pierce County near Merson Road and Crump Road. It crossed the Big Satilla Creek, entering into Appling County at this point, and continued on a northeast track. The funnel may have briefly lifted off the ground at this point. The next significant damage occurred along Metts-Overstreet Road with the complete destruction of a singlewide mobile home and a farm shed at approximately 1425EST. Additionally, at this location, a brick cider block structure had one wall knocked over. The tornado then moved into a dairy just west of the intersection of State Road 15 and Wireglass Road. The event was rated as an EF-2 with winds of 135 mph at this location. Three metal building structures were destroyed with one removed from its foundation and blown into a pile of scrap metal. It is possible the funnel may have approached EF-3 at this particular location with winds of 140 to 150 mph; however, other damage to frame and masonry structures at the dairy suggested a lower wind value should be assigned to this area. One individual escaped injury by jumping into a depressed area inside one of the structures before it blew down around her. East of State Road 15, along Wireglass Road, extensive tree blow down and snapping continued in a tree farm. Once again the funnel likely lifted off the ground touching down again at 1430EST near 4230 Antioch Church Road destroying a doublewide mobile home. At this location, two middle aged individuals were in the bedroom, preparing for work, when the funnel destroyed the mobile home. Both escaped with only minor cuts despite the fact that the doublewide was completely obliterated and the frames bent. Once again at this location the winds were rated at 135 mph for a strong EF-2 rating.
