EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record flooding continued to expand eastern the eastern suburbs of the Atlanta metropolitan area as renewed rounds of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain tracked repeatedly over the same areas throughout the day.  Portions of hundreds of roads in the county were closed from flooding and washed out bridges and/or culverts.  Hundreds of homes, apartments, and businesses sustained significant flood damage.  Record flooding was occurring on Suwanee Creek near Suwanee.  Some of the most significant flooding occurred in the Stone Mountain area on U.S. Highway 78 from the DeKalb county line to Ross Road.  Monetary damage to property and roads during this time frame across Gwinnett county was estimated to be near $12.5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep full latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the system.  An unseasonably warm, moist air mass was present across Georgia in advance of this system.  Widespread moderate to heavy rain tracked repeatedly across the northern 1/4th of the state throughout much of the day in advance of the main weather system.  Toward evening, a line of strong thunderstorms entered the northwest part of the state, spawning a brief F0 tornado in Chattooga county.  While the line of thunderstorms continued to progress across the state, it weakened through the evening, but intensified after midnight as it moved across west central and central Georgia spawning another brief tornado in Monroe county during the early morning hours.  Other isolated strong thunderstorms caused wind damage. During the afternoon of the 7th, strong northwest winds spread across the region behind the cold front, blowing down several trees in north Georgia, injuring one person in Whitfield county in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS gage on the Etowah River, 1.5 miles northeast of Canton near Interstate-575, reached flood stage of 17 feet at 1220 pm EST on January 6th and continued at or above flood stage until 702 pm EST.  In addition, the Little River at Georgia Highway 5, two miles northeast of Woodstock, reached flood stage of 11.0 feet briefly at 230 pm EST on January 6th.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area.  Eleven deaths were recorded during the event, mostly vehicle related deaths at night from washed out bridges and roads as a result of swollen creeks.  Property damages are estimated to be at least $0.5 Billion, with hundreds of homes and businesses destroyed by flood waters.  Bridges on several state and local highways were washed out and some will take months to even a year to replace.   Eighteen counties (Bartow, Carroll, Chattooga, Cherokee, Cobb, Coweta, DeKalb, Douglas, Fulton, Gwinnett, Heard, Newton, Paulding, Rockdale, Stephens, and Walker) received a presidential disaster declaration as a result of the historic floods.  Twenty-one counties were declared eligible for public assistance, including several central Georgia counties that were affected by flash flooding earlier in the period.||Some of the worst flooding was observed along Sweetwater Creek near the Cobb, Fulton, Douglas county line.  Hundreds of homes were flooded and destroyed as the waters of Sweetwater Creek rose more than 20 feet above flood level.||Many main stem and secondary creeks, streams, and rivers across north and central Georgia, particularly in northwest and west central Georgia, reached record flood levels.  Many of these same creeks, streams, and rivers remained in flood for several days.  In some cases, all time record flood levels were recorded. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although flash flooding began to subside, extensive flooding was reported across the county and confirmed from several USGS stream gages.  Flooding expanded on areas earlier dexperiencing flash flooding.  Record flooding was observed along Sweetwater Creek near Powder Springs, Powder Springs Creek, Noses Creek, and numerous other small creeks and tributaries.  At least 100 roads across the county were closed, including portions of Interstate 20 and Interstate 285 near Six Flags.  Extensive rescue operations were conducted in many areas of the county as motorists found their vehicle submerged in flood waters.  At least a dozen people were evacuated in Powder Springs because of rising water from Sweetwater Creek near their homes.  Other neighborhoods in south Cobb county were also flooded.  Damage to home and businesses throughout much of the county was tremendous.  During this time period alone, it was estimated that roads and property in the county sustained damage nearing $6 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ida, which formed in the Caribbean and intensified to a category two hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson scale, moved north from the Caribbean across the central Gulf of Mexico and then inland along the U.S. Gulf coast near Mobile, Alabama early on the 10th.  The remnants of the hurricane then moved east-northeast across southern Alabama and southern Georgia before moving off the east coast of the U.S. as a strong surface low pressure area.  Heavy rainfall, on the order of four to five inches, was common across north Georgia, with one to two inch amounts across central areas.  With the ground totally saturated from several prior months of heavy rainfall, widespread creek, stream, and river flooding was observed over a two to three day period.  Because the rain intensity was mostly moderate or less and extended over a period of 18-24 hours, flash flooding events were isolated, with most of the flood events being of the creek, stream, and river flood nature.||The small wind core of Hurricane Ida and its track across land significantly reduced the wind effects of this system on north and central Georgia.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 20 mph range with a few stronger gusts.  Only Banks county in northeast Georgia observed any wind damage, where a few trees and power lines were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on Lookout Creek at New England reached its flood stage of 12.0 feet.  This resulted in minor flooding adjacent to the creek.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal in these areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding continued across much of Gwinnett county during this period.  Several creeks and streams were in at least moderate flood from record rainfall.  The USGS stream gage on Shoal Creek near Lawrenceville exceeded its flood stage of 11 feet for several hours, beginning at 459 pm EDT, and the Apalachee River near Dacula exceeded its flood stage of 8 feet for several hours beginning at 604 pm EDT.  The Alcovy River, just southeast of Lawrenceville, went into moderate flood during this time period.  Additional monetary damage in excess of $600,000, primarily to roads, was estimated to have occurred during this time frame.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County Emergency Management Director reported that significant and extensive flooding continued across much of the county.  The Chattooga River breached the levees and flooded parts of the city of Trion.  Dozens of homes and business were flooded and sustained significant damage, especially in the city of Trion.  A number of roads were also flooded, impassable, and closed.  Additional monetary damage nearing $7.5 million was incurred during this time frame.

Significant flooding was ongoing in the Rossville, Fort Oglethorpe, and LaFayette areas.  At least 20 roads were closed because of flooding.  Dozens of homes were flooded and suffered significant damage.  Additional monetary damage in the county from flooding during this time frame were estimated to be near $6 million.

Hundreds of homes were flooded and destroyed as the waters of Sweetwater Creek rose more than 20 feet above flood level.||Many main stem and secondary creeks, streams, and rivers across north and central Georgia, particularly in northwest and west central Georgia, reached record flood levels.  Many of these same creeks, streams, and rivers remained in flood for several days.  In some cases, all time record flood levels were recorded.  The following is a list of new record flood levels set on several creeks, streams, and rivers in north and west Georgia.  Noonday Creek near Woodstock rose to 19.6 feet on the 21st at 530 pm EDT. The North Fork of Peachtree Creek at Atlanta rose to 18.07 feet on the 21st at 715 pm EDT.  The previous record was 17.70 feet set on September 16, 2004.  Utoy Creek near Atlanta rose to 27.04 feet on the 22nd at 1000 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.86 feet set on May 6, 2003.  The Chattahoochee River at Whitesburg rose to 29.61 feet on the 21st at 745 pm EDT.  The previous record was 29.11 feet on December 11, 1919.  Suwanee Creek at Suwanee rose to 14.30 feet on the 21st at 645 pm EDT.  The previous record was 12.04 feet set on October 5, 1996.  The Yellow River at Lithonia rose to 25.50 feet on the 22nd at 515 am EDT.  The previous record was 17.53 feet set on May 7, 2003.  The Yellow River near Conyers below Milstead rose to 22.54 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.36 feet set on July 8, 2005.  The Chattahoochee River at Franklin rose to 29.98 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 28.40 feet set on December 15, 1919.  The Sweetwater Creek at Austell rose to 30.17 feet on the 22nd at 1000 pm EDT. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Earlier flash flooding evolved into record and catastrophic flooding as confirmed by emergency management personnel and USGS stream gages.  More than 40 roads across the county were completely washed out, including Post Road just south of Interstate-20.  A portion of Interstate-20 near Fairburn Road was closed for nearly 12-hours causing major traffic backups throughout the region.  The main line of the Norfolk-Southern railroad near Villa Rica was also washed out.  A seventh person in Douglas county fell victim to the raging flood waters.  A 29-year old female perished when she was swept away in her vehicle by flood waters from the Dog River at 451 am EDT.  Over 1200 residents in the county were left without power as several tress in water logged soils fell on adjacent power lines.  This primarily affected areas near and including Arbor Place Mall along Interstate-20 near Fairburn Road.  Schools throughout the county were closed because of many impassable and flooded roads.  Many homes, businesses, and roads across the county sustained tremendous damage.  Washed out bridges will result in some roads being closed for up to a year, including portions of Georgia Highway 166 near Campbellton.  Monetary damage to roads and property during this time frame was estimated to be close to $9 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial flash flooding evolved into a record and catastrophic flood event during this time frame.  Carroll county emergency management personnel reported that at least a dozen other people were rescued from flooded homes and cars around the county, including one woman who escaped to safety as her vehicle was washed as she was driving.  Substantial damage to homes, businesses, and roads were observed.  Monetary damage during this time period was estimated to be in excess of $16 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep full latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the system.  An unseasonably warm, moist air mass was present across Georgia in advance of this system.  Widespread moderate to heavy rain tracked repeatedly across the northern 1/4th of the state throughout much of the day in advance of the main weather system.  Toward evening, a line of strong thunderstorms entered the northwest part of the state, spawning a brief F0 tornado in Chattooga county.  While the line of thunderstorms continued to progress across the state, it weakened through the evening, but intensified after midnight as it moved across west central and central Georgia spawning another brief tornado in Monroe county during the early morning hours.  Other isolated strong thunderstorms caused wind damage. During the afternoon of the 7th, strong northwest winds spread across the region behind the cold front, blowing down several trees in north Georgia, injuring one person in Whitfield county in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia determined that flooding occurred on Racoon Creek at Hair Lake Road, and Spring Creek at Spring Creek Road.  Small streams also overflowed their banks at Fish Hatchery Road and Ragland Road.  In addition, the Chattooga River went into minor flood until the January 8th at 232 AM EST.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that a brief EF0 tornado touched down four miles east-southeast of Chattoogaville and moved north-northeast paralleling Georgia Highway 100 for just a little over a 2-mile long track.  Spotty tree damage was observed along Old Holland Road and Georgia Highway 100 along the tornado track.  Light to moderate damage was observed to the roofs of three buildings and several outbuildings, some of which may have been a product of straight-line winds.

The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area.  Eleven deaths were recorded during the event, mostly vehicle related deaths at night from washed out bridges and roads as a result of swollen creeks.  Property damages are estimated to be at least $0.5 Billion, with hundreds of homes and businesses destroyed by flood waters.  Bridges on several state and local highways were washed out and some will take months to even a year to replace.   Eighteen counties (Bartow, Carroll, Chattooga, Cherokee, Cobb, Coweta, DeKalb, Douglas, Fulton, Gwinnett, Heard, Newton, Paulding, Rockdale, Stephens, and Walker) received a presidential disaster declaration as a result of the historic floods.  Twenty-one counties were declared eligible for public assistance, including several central Georgia counties that were affected by flash flooding earlier in the period.||Some of the worst flooding was observed along Sweetwater Creek near the Cobb, Fulton, Douglas county line.  Hundreds of homes were flooded and destroyed as the waters of Sweetwater Creek rose more than 20 feet above flood level.||Many main stem and secondary creeks, streams, and rivers across north and central Georgia, particularly in northwest and west central Georgia, reached record flood levels.  Many of these same creeks, streams, and rivers remained in flood for several days.  In some cases, all time record flood levels were recorded.  The following is a list of new record flood levels set on several creeks, streams, and rivers in north and west Georgia.  Noonday Creek near Woodstock rose to 19.6 feet on the 21st at 530 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.30 feet set on July 11, 2005.  Nickajack Creek at Mableton reached 19.30 feet on the 22nd at 215 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.60 feet set on July 11, 2005.  The North Fork of Peachtree Creek at Atlanta rose to 18.07 feet on the 21st at 715 pm EDT.  The previous record was 17.70 feet set on September 16, 2004.  Utoy Creek near Atlanta rose to 27.04 feet on the 22nd at 1000 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.86 feet set on May 6, 2003.  The Chattahoochee River at Whitesburg rose to 29.61 feet on the 21st at 745 pm EDT.  The previous record was 29.11 feet on December 11, 1919.  Suwanee Creek at Suwanee rose to 14.30 feet on the 21st at 645 pm EDT.  The previous record was 12.04 feet set on October 5, 1996.  The Yellow River at Lithonia rose to 25.50 feet on the 22nd at 515 am EDT.  The previous record was 17.53 feet set on May 7, 2003.  The Yellow River near Conyers below Milstead rose to 22.54 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.36 feet set on July 8, 2005.  The Chattahoochee River at Franklin rose to 29.98 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 28.40 feet set on December 15, 1919.  The Sweetwater Creek at Austell rose to 30.17 feet on the 22nd at 1000 pm EDT.  The previous record was 21.81 feet on July 12, 2005.||The following rivers reached flood levels that were within the top five all time levels at these locations.  Peachtree Creek in Atlanta rose to 23.89 feet on the 21st at 915 pm EDT.  The all time record is 25.80 feet recorded on December 20th, 1919.  Nancy Creek in Atlanta reached 14.69 feet on the 21st at 930 pm EDT.  The all time record is 15.50 feet on December 1, 1973.  The Chattahoochee River at Vinings reached 28.10 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1919.  The South Fork of the Peachtree Creek reached 15.21 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1976.  The Chattahoochee River at Campbellton reached 30.55 feet on the 22nd at 300 pm EDT.  The all time record is 31.60 feet set on December 10, 1919.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cherokee county emergency management personnel and USGS stream gages confirmed that major to record flooding continued across much of the county.  Hundreds of homes, apartments, businesses, and roads sustained damages from flooding.  A number of culverts and/or bridges were washed out and several roads were closed, including portions of Interstate-575 near Woodstock.  Flood waters began to recede after 830 am EDT on the 22nd, but many locations remained in major to record flood through 830 am EDT on the 23rd.  Monetary damage from flooding  continued to mount, with damages exceeding $44.5 million during this time frame, one of the highest flood damage amounts reported during this period of historical flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Arctic air mass spread across north and central Georgia during the later half of the month.  Rainfall was well below normal during this period.  Soil and ground conditions were quite dry.  A couple of small wildfires in far north Georgia had to be contained by the Georgia Forestry Department.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported that a human caused wildfire had to be contained at Dockery Gap.  Approximately 0.16 acres of forest were burned.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding continued and expanded from southern into western Cobb county during this time frame.  USGS stream gages showed that a number of creeks and streams across western and southern Cobb county were already in flood or rose to and exceeded flood stage during this time frame.  This included Powder Springs Creek, Noses Creek, and Sweetwater Creek.  Heavy rain and thunderstorms continued to track repeatedly from southwest to northeast across the same areas moving the area closer and closer to the record and historical flooding that unfolded during the early morning hours of the 21st.  During this time period, an increasing number of roads and culverts were damaged from the rising flood waters.  Damage during this time period across western Cobb county was estimated to exceed $200,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catoosa County Emergency Management Director reported flash flooding across the northern half of the county, especially in the Lakeview area.  Two people had to be rescued by boat from their homes as creeks rapidly rose out of their banks in the area.  Several apartments were flooded and sustained significant damage.  A number of cars were submerged in flood waters as well.  However, no injuries or deaths were reported from flash flooding in this county.  Monetary damage was mostly in the form of flooded apartments, homes, and vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: USGS stream gages along the Chattahoochee River, which flows from northeast to southwest through Heard county and near Franklin, indicated that major to record flooding was occurring and continued for several days.  However, flooding was confined to areas adjacent to the river and no significant road or property damage was reported.  Monetary damage was limited to minor debris removal near the river.

In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area.  Eleven deaths were recorded during the event, mostly vehicle related deaths at night from washed out bridges and roads as a result of swollen creeks.  Property damages are estimated to be at least $0.5 Billion, with hundreds of homes and businesses destroyed by flood waters.  Bridges on several state and local highways were washed out and some will take months to even a year to replace.   Eighteen counties (Bartow, Carroll, Chattooga, Cherokee, Cobb, Coweta, DeKalb, Douglas, Fulton, Gwinnett, Heard, Newton, Paulding, Rockdale, Stephens, and Walker) received a presidential disaster declaration as a result of the historic floods.  Twenty-one counties were declared eligible for public assistance, including several central Georgia counties that were affected by flash flooding earlier in the period.||Some of the worst flooding was observed along Sweetwater Creek near the Cobb, Fulton, Douglas county line.  Hundreds of homes were flooded and destroyed as the waters of Sweetwater Creek rose more than 20 feet above flood level.||Many main stem and secondary creeks, streams, and rivers across north and central Georgia, particularly in northwest and west central Georgia, reached record flood levels.  Many of these same creeks, streams, and rivers remained in flood for several days.  In some cases, all time record flood levels were recorded.  The following is a list of new record flood levels set on several creeks, streams, and rivers in north and west Georgia.  Noonday Creek near Woodstock rose to 19.6 feet on the 21st at 530 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.30 feet set on July 11, 2005.  Nickajack Creek at Mableton reached 19.30 feet on the 22nd at 215 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.60 feet set on July 11, 2005.  The North Fork of Peachtree Creek at Atlanta rose to 18.07 feet on the 21st at 715 pm EDT.  The previous record was 17.70 feet set on September 16, 2004.  Utoy Creek near Atlanta rose to 27.04 feet on the 22nd at 1000 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.86 feet set on May 6, 2003.  The Chattahoochee River at Whitesburg rose to 29.61 feet on the 21st at 745 pm EDT.  The previous record was 29.11 feet on December 11, 1919.  Suwanee Creek at Suwanee rose to 14.30 feet on the 21st at 645 pm EDT.  The previous record was 12.04 feet set on October 5, 1996.  The Yellow River at Lithonia rose to 25.50 feet on the 22nd at 515 am EDT.  The previous record was 17.53 feet set on May 7, 2003.  The Yellow River near Conyers below Milstead rose to 22.54 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.36 feet set on July 8, 2005.  The Chattahoochee River at Franklin rose to 29.98 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 28.40 feet set on December 15, 1919.  The Sweetwater Creek at Austell rose to 30.17 feet on the 22nd at 1000 pm EDT.  The previous record was 21.81 feet on July 12, 2005.||The following rivers reached flood levels that were within the top five all time levels at these locations.  Peachtree Creek in Atlanta rose to 23.89 feet on the 21st at 915 pm EDT.  The all time record is 25.80 feet recorded on December 20th, 1919.  Nancy Creek in Atlanta reached 14.69 feet on the 21st at 930 pm EDT.  The all time record is 15.50 feet on December 1, 1973.  The Chattahoochee River at Vinings reached 28.10 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1919.  The South Fork of the Peachtree Creek reached 15.21 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1976.  The Chattahoochee River at Campbellton reached 30.55 feet on the 22nd at 300 pm EDT.  The all time record is 31.60 feet set on December 10, 1919.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several USGS stream gages showed significant flooding occurring across Cobb county as the record and catastrophic flash flooding continued to unfold across the county as repeated rounds of thunderstorms with heavy rain tracked from southwest to northwest across the area.  Rainfall amounts in excess of 10 inches were observed in much of the county during the nighttime and early morning hours.  Sope Creek near Marietta reached flood stage of 12 feet at 238 am EDT, Nickajack Creek near Mableton reached its flood stage of 10 feet at 504 am EDT, and the Chattahoochee River near Vinings and Cobb Parkway reached its flood stage of 14 feet at 346 am EDT.  Many roads adjacent these rivers and creeks suffered significant flooding, became impassable and were closed.  Many other smaller creeks and streams in these same general areas also went into flood.  Monetary damage to roads during this time frame was estimated at $10,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low pressure area and associated cold front were moving from the mid-south into the southeast U.S.  A backdoor cold front was located across northeast Georgia.  A warm, tropical air mass was present between the advancing cold front to the west and the backdoor front across northeast Georgia.  Upper-level forcing was strong, thanks to the deep upper low moving into the southeast U.S.  These systems combined to bring a 24-hour period of heavy rain and thunderstorms that progressed from northwest to southeast across north and central Georgia.  With this event falling only 6 days after the record and catastrophic flooding that affected west central and north central Georgia on the 20th and 21st, flash flooding was a major concern.  Fortunately, the activity moved through more quickly, but additional flash flooding and river flooding was observed.  In addition, as the afternoon progressed and the activity moved into the central and east central portions of the state, an increasingly warm and unstable air mass was encountered, resulting in several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington County 911 Center reported that from 15 to 20 trees were blown down in the Sandersville area, near the county 911 center.  Some businesses in Sandersville sustained at least minor damage from downed trees.  In addition, a metal carport was blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cobb county emergency management personnel and USGS stream gages confirmed that record to catastrophic flooding continued across much of the county.  Hundreds of homes, apartments, businesses, and roads sustained damages from flooding.  Many culverts and/or bridges were washed out and several roads were closed.  Some of the worst flooding during this event occurred in the Cobb and Douglas county area.  Flood waters began to recede after 830 am EDT on the 22nd, but many locations remained in major to record flood through 715 am EDT on the 23rd.  Monetary damage from flooding continued to mount, with damage during this time frame exceeding $12.5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad cutoff upper low, which had been located over central Texas, was beginning to move very slowly east by the 16th.  This upper low, in combination with unusually deep tropical moisture across the region for mid-September, began an extensive period of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain.  The deep tropical moisture was largely the result of a persistent 850 mb flow from the Gulf of Mexico.  The pattern changed very little for nearly a week as the upper low drifted very slowly east, completely cutoff from the main belt of westerlies, which were located well to the north in southern Canada.  The first flash flood event of an extended period of historical and record flooding across north and central Georgia, began on the 15th in Gilmer county.  A few storms became strong to marginally severe as well.||A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event began to unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta. In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during this time, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily across parts of north and central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on Big Creek in far southwest Forsyth county, along the Fulton county line, reached flood stage of 7.0 feet at 430 pm EDT.  The creek crested at 7.17 feet at 530 pm EDT, then fell below flood stage at 722 pm EDT.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal adjacent to the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area.  Eleven deaths were recorded during the event, mostly vehicle related deaths at night from washed out bridges and roads as a result of swollen creeks.  Property damages are estimated to be at least $0.5 Billion, with hundreds of homes and businesses destroyed by flood waters.  Bridges on several state and local highways were washed out and some will take months to even a year to replace. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through north and central Georgia in the wake of the storm system that brought substantial rain and some severe weather to the area on the 6th.  Strong northwest winds developed early in the day across the northern sections of the state and continued well into the afternoon.  The strong gradient wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph blew down a number of trees and some power lines in north Georgia counties and resulted in at least two minor injuries.  The strongest winds accompanied a line of showers along the cold front that moved through north and central Georgia at midday.  Below is a summary of wind speeds observed from airport and University of Georgia mesonet stations, as well as a list of reported damages by county.||Winder, (Barrow county) - 44 mph, Macon (Bibb county) - 45 mph, Athens (Clarke county) - 36 mph, Atlanta Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport and Jonesboro (Clayton) - 38 and 35 mph, respectively, Kennesaw and Marietta (Cobb county) - 39 and 40 mph, respectively, Chamblee (DeKalb county) - 37 mph, Unadilla and Vienna (Dooly county) - 47 and 40 mph, respectively, Peachtree City (Fayette) - 38 mph, Rome (Floyd county) - 33 mph, Blue Ridge (Fannin county) - 46 mph, Calhoun (Gordon county) - 36 mph, Gainesville (Hall county) - 43 mph, Roopville (Heard county) - 37 mph, Warner Robins (Houston county) - 41 mph, Dublin (Laurens county) - 35 mph, Dahlonega (Lumpkin county) - 46 mph, Columbus (Muscogee county) - 38 mph, Covington (Newton county) - 44 mph, Watkinsville (Oconee county) - 47 mph, Fort Valley and Byron (Peach county) - 42 and 35 mph, respectively, Eatonton (Putnam county) - 38 mph, Griffin (Spalding county) - 36 mph, Plains (Sumter county) - 49 mph, Vidalia (Toombs county) - 41 mph, LaGrange (Troup county) - 38 mph, and LaFayette (Walker county) - 40 mph.||Banks county - Numerous trees and several power lines were down on roads across the county.  Bibb county - Several trees and power lines were down across the county.  Some traffic signals blown down.  One tree was down on a vehicle.  Clarke county - a number of trees, several power lines, and some traffic signals were down, especially in the city of Athens.  DeKalb county - live power lines were knocked down onto a school bus carrying 30 elementary children.  At least 5,000 people in the county were without power.  Fulton county - a few trees and power lines were down within the county.  Henry county - Four trees were down just north of McDonough.  Jackson county - a power pole was blown onto a mobile home.  The home was consequently set on fire.  Jefferson - Strong winds blew a tree over onto a mobile home on Railroad Avenue in Gibson.  The tree fell through the roof of the home trapping a woman and her 3-year old boy in a recliner where they had been watching television.  Although rescue personnel were able to remove them from under the tree, the woman suffered a broken hip.  Oglethorpe county - the canopy collapsed and destroyed two gas pumps at a convenience store on U.S. Highway 78.  Two gas pumps were destroyed as a result.  Whitfield county - a person was injured when struck in the head by a falling tree limb on LaFayette Road just southwest of Rocky Face.  In addition, an unoccupied house was destroyed by a tree and at least five other trees were blown down within the county.||Scattered power outages from downed trees and power lines were also reported in Baldwin, Crawford, Houston, and Taylor counties.  No specific damages were provided from these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon Telegraph reported that strong winds associated with a line of showers blew down several trees and trees down onto power lines across the county.  One tree fell down on a vehicle.  Scattered power outages were reported in Bibb and surrounding counties of Baldwin, Crawford, Houston, and Taylor as well.

Hundreds of homes were flooded and destroyed as the waters of Sweetwater Creek rose more than 20 feet above flood level.||Many main stem and secondary creeks, streams, and rivers across north and central Georgia, particularly in northwest and west central Georgia, reached record flood levels.  Many of these same creeks, streams, and rivers remained in flood for several days.  In some cases, all time record flood levels were recorded.  The following is a list of new record flood levels set on several creeks, streams, and rivers in north and west Georgia.  Noonday Creek near Woodstock rose to 19.6 feet on the 21st at 530 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.30 feet set on July 11, 2005.  Nickajack Creek at Mableton reached 19.30 feet on the 22nd at 215 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.60 feet set on July 11, 2005.  The North Fork of Peachtree Creek at Atlanta rose to 18.07 feet on the 21st at 715 pm EDT.  The previous record was 17.70 feet set on September 16, 2004.  Utoy Creek near Atlanta rose to 27.04 feet on the 22nd at 1000 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.86 feet set on May 6, 2003.  The Chattahoochee River at Whitesburg rose to 29.61 feet on the 21st at 745 pm EDT.  The previous record was 29.11 feet on December 11, 1919.  Suwanee Creek at Suwanee rose to 14.30 feet on the 21st at 645 pm EDT.  The previous record was 12.04 feet set on October 5, 1996.  The Yellow River at Lithonia rose to 25.50 feet on the 22nd at 515 am EDT.  The previous record was 17.53 feet set on May 7, 2003.  The Yellow River near Conyers below Milstead rose to 22.54 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.36 feet set on July 8, 2005.  The Chattahoochee River at Franklin rose to 29.98 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 28.40 feet set on December 15, 1919.  The Sweetwater Creek at Austell rose to 30.17 feet on the 22nd at 1000 pm EDT.  The previous record was 21.81 feet on July 12, 2005.||The following rivers reached flood levels that were within the top five all time levels at these locations.  Peachtree Creek in Atlanta rose to 23.89 feet on the 21st at 915 pm EDT.  The all time record is 25.80 feet recorded on December 20th, 1919. The Chattahoochee River at Vinings reached 28.10 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1919.  The South Fork of the Peachtree Creek reached 15.21 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1976.  The Chattahoochee River at Campbellton reached 30.55 feet on the 22nd at 300 pm EDT.  The all time record is 31.60 feet set on December 10, 1919.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Paulding county Emergency Management personnel reported that flash flooding continued to expand and worsen across much of the county during this time frame.  Multiple road closures and a number of flash flood and high water rescues were conducted during this time frame.  However, no deaths were reported in Paulding county from the flash flooding.  Damage to roads and bridges continued to expand||As repeated thunderstorms with heavy rain tracked across the area from the southwest, numerous major roads were blocked by high water, including U.S. Highway 278 and Georgia Highway 92 near Hiram.  More than 69 roads were eventually closed across the county.  The county court house and at least two convenience stores on Georgia Highway 92 were closed because of flooding.  Schools were closed for several days because of flooded roads.||County emergency management personnel reported that 37 vehicles were rescued from flood waters and 46 residents of the county fell victim to rising flood waters and had to be rescued from peril.  Fifteen individuals were removed from their homes because of rising flood waters.  Two residents were electrocuted in flood waters by lightning strikes, but were not killed.  Three homes suffered wall collapses from flood waters.  At least eight gas leaks were caused by flood waters and eight power lines were either brought down or affected by trees downed in water logged soils.||During this time frame, flood damage was estimated to be $200,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walker county emergency management personnel reported flash flooding across the northern half of the county.  The first floor of the Battlewood Apartments in Fort Oglethorpe were flooded, resulting in evacuations of several residents.  Several roads were flooded and closed as creeks rose several feet out of their banks.  Monetary damage in the county was primarily a result of damaged roads, bridges, and culverts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that flash floods in Carroll county took the life of a 2-year old boy.  The mobile home of a family of four was swept away by flood waters from Snake Creek.  The childs's body was found almost a mile away from where the trailer home was originally anchored.  The mobile home was located in a trailer park near Snake Creek, which was observed to have been nearly 20 feet deep.  Local law enforcement officials note that the creek is normally only at a depth of two to three feet.  It took nearly four hours to rescue the three other residents of the home, a man, wife, and 1-year old boy, which were found clinging to trees several yards downstream.  This initial flash flood marked the beginning of a period of historical and record flash flooding across several west central Georgia counties.  Flood damage during this initial time frame was estimated to be in excess of $1.3 million.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record and historical flooding began to expand into Cobb county during this time frame as repeated thunderstorms streamed from southwest to northeast across the region.  The USGS stream gage on Sweetwater Creek near Powder Springs and the Cobb/Paulding county line began to show flash flooding expanding eastward into southwest Cobb county.  The creek exceeded the flood stage of 13 feet at 1033 pm EST and continued to rise for sometime thereafter.  Flooding of some roads and culverts began during this time period.  Flood damage during this time frame was estimated to be near $12,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous emergency management sources, as well as local law enforcement, newspapers, broadcast media, and National Weather Service surveys confirmed that record and catastrophic flash flooding began across Douglas county during this time frame.  Repeated rounds of thunderstorms with heavy rain tracked from southwest to northeast.  Flash flooding was observed in most parts of the county during this time frame.  Up to six inches of rain fell in this area on already saturated ground.  Rain continued overnight beyond this time frame, with some parts of the county receiving nearly 22 inches by the afternoon of the 21st.  Hundreds of homes and businesses were impacted before the flooding subsided, many totally destroyed.  Dozens of roads and bridges were were washed out as well, including some major roads such as Georgia Highway 166.  Some roads, including Georgia Highway 166 will be closed for up to a year while washed out bridges are repaired.  In addition, Douglas county suffered six flash flood deaths during this event, the most of any county from the historical floods in late September 2009.  Flash flood deaths are documented as follows.  The first flash flood death occurred at 1030 pm EDT when a 44-year old female was attempting to cross Mobley Creek at Banks Mill Road and was swept away in her Jeep Liberty.  The second flash flood death occurred at 1100 pm EDT when a 29-year old male was swept way in his sport utility vehicle while attempting to cross Pool Creek at Poole Mill Road.  The third flash flood death was that of a 26-year old female who also was attempting to cross Mobley Creek at Banks Mill Road.  The fourth flash flood death was that of a 27-year old male attempting to cross Town Branch Creek at Mann Road.  The fifth flash flood death in Douglas county occurred where Stewart's Mill Road crosses Anneewakee Creek when a 49-year old nurse was swept away in her vehicle while attempting to cross the creek.  She was on her way to work at the time.  The sixth flash flood death in Douglas county was that of a 55-year old male.  He was swept away in his Ford Ranger pickup truck while attempting to cross West Bully Creek on North Helton Road.  Numerous other high water rescues took place.  Many took place via boat to rescue people from flood homes, businesses, and vehicles.||During this time frame, damage was estimated to be close to $1 million.  Damages rose into the millions overnight and Monday as the flooding continued and worsened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep full latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the system.  An unseasonably warm, moist air mass was present across Georgia in advance of this system.  Widespread moderate to heavy rain tracked repeatedly across the northern 1/4th of the state throughout much of the day in advance of the main weather system.  Toward evening, a line of strong thunderstorms entered the northwest part of the state, spawning a brief F0 tornado in Chattooga county.  While the line of thunderstorms continued to progress across the state, it weakened through the evening, but intensified after midnight as it moved across west central and central Georgia spawning another brief tornado in Monroe county during the early morning hours.  Other isolated strong thunderstorms caused wind damage. During the afternoon of the 7th, strong northwest winds spread across the region behind the cold front, blowing down several trees in north Georgia, injuring one person in Whitfield county in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County Emergency Management Director reported that the Etowah River had risen out of it banks at Old Federal Road and Nicholson Road in the northwest part of the county.  Nicholson Road was inundated with at least a foot of water near Bannister Creek.  In addition, Setting Down Creek was flooding at Concord and Oak Grove Roads.  Six roads were closed as a result of these flooding incidents.  Windermere Park in the southeast part of the county was also closed as a consequence of flooding from the Etowah River.  Damage was limited to debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of polar and Arctic cold fronts moved through north and central Georgia during mid-January.  Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts were observed behind several of these fronts.  These strong winds caused minor damage in Madison county on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Madison County Journal of Danielsville reported that strong winds ripped off part of the tin roof of a home on Georgia Highway 191 in Danielsville.  Parts of the tin roof were found in a nearby oak tree, some drive into the side of the tree like stakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Catoosa county emergency management personnel reported that extensive flooding was observed across much of the county as repeated thunderstorms with heavy rain tracked across the area for several hours.  Many roads became flooded and impassable and suffered considerable damage.  A number of home and apartments were flooded, especially in the Fort Oglethorpe and Midway areas.  Schools were closed for at least two days because of many impassable roads.  Monetary property damage during this time frame was estimated to be $340,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County Emergency Management Director reported significant flooding across the county.  Several roads in the county had water flowing across them as creeks exceeded flood stage in many locations.  Some roads had to be closed at creek crossings.  The most significant flooding was reported north of Cloudland.  Schools were closed in the county for at least two days because of impassable roads.  Monetary road damage was estimated to be at least $60,000, mostly a result of erosion.

Toward evening, a line of strong thunderstorms entered the northwest part of the state, spawning a brief F0 tornado in Chattooga county.  While the line of thunderstorms continued to progress across the state, it weakened through the evening, but intensified after midnight as it moved across west central and central Georgia spawning another brief tornado in Monroe county during the early morning hours.  Other isolated strong thunderstorms caused wind damage. During the afternoon of the 7th, strong northwest winds spread across the region behind the cold front, blowing down several trees in north Georgia, injuring one person in Whitfield county in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Tribune News of Cartersville reported that four roads in the county were closed because of flooding.   These included Parr Wade Bishop and Old Grassdale Roads in Cartersville, Puckett Road in Emerson, and Euharlee Road in the Mission Ridge Subdivision.  The later of which was a result of a collapsed retaining wall.  Damage was limited to minor debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area.  Eleven deaths were recorded during the event, mostly vehicle related deaths at night from washed out bridges and roads as a result of swollen creeks.  Property damages are estimated to be at least $0.5 Billion, with hundreds of homes and businesses destroyed by flood waters.  Bridges on several state and local highways were washed out and some will take months to even a year to replace.   Eighteen counties (Bartow, Carroll, Chattooga, Cherokee, Cobb, Coweta, DeKalb, Douglas, Fulton, Gwinnett, Heard, Newton, Paulding, Rockdale, Stephens, and Walker) received a presidential disaster declaration as a result of the historic floods.  Twenty-one counties were declared eligible for public assistance, including several central Georgia counties that were affected by flash flooding earlier in the period.||Some of the worst flooding was observed along Sweetwater Creek near the Cobb, Fulton, Douglas county line.  Hundreds of homes were flooded and destroyed as the waters of Sweetwater Creek rose more than 20 feet above flood level.||Many main stem and secondary creeks, streams, and rivers across north and central Georgia, particularly in northwest and west central Georgia, reached record flood levels.  Many of these same creeks, streams, and rivers remained in flood for several days.  In some cases, all time record flood levels were recorded.  The following is a list of new record flood levels set on several creeks, streams, and rivers in north and west Georgia.  Noonday Creek near Woodstock rose to 19.6 feet on the 21st at 530 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.30 feet set on July 11, 2005.  Nickajack Creek at Mableton reached 19.30 feet on the 22nd at 215 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.60 feet set on July 11, 2005.  The North Fork of Peachtree Creek at Atlanta rose to 18.07 feet on the 21st at 715 pm EDT.  The previous record was 17.70 feet set on September 16, 2004.  Utoy Creek near Atlanta rose to 27.04 feet on the 22nd at 1000 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.86 feet set on May 6, 2003.  The Chattahoochee River at Whitesburg rose to 29.61 feet on the 21st at 745 pm EDT.  The previous record was 29.11 feet on December 11, 1919.  Suwanee Creek at Suwanee rose to 14.30 feet on the 21st at 645 pm EDT.  The previous record was 12.04 feet set on October 5, 1996.  The Yellow River at Lithonia rose to 25.50 feet on the 22nd at 515 am EDT.  The previous record was 17.53 feet set on May 7, 2003.  The Yellow River near Conyers below Milstead rose to 22.54 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.36 feet set on July 8, 2005.  The Chattahoochee River at Franklin rose to 29.98 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 28.40 feet set on December 15, 1919.  The Sweetwater Creek at Austell rose to 30.17 feet on the 22nd at 1000 pm EDT.  The previous record was 21.81 feet on July 12, 2005.||The following rivers reached flood levels that were within the top five all time levels at these locations.  Peachtree Creek in Atlanta rose to 23.89 feet on the 21st at 915 pm EDT.  The all time record is 25.80 feet recorded on December 20th, 1919.  Nancy Creek in Atlanta reached 14.69 feet on the 21st at 930 pm EDT.  The all time record is 15.50 feet on December 1, 1973.  The Chattahoochee River at Vinings reached 28.10 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1919.  The South Fork of the Peachtree Creek reached 15.21 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1976.  The Chattahoochee River at Campbellton reached 30.55 feet on the 22nd at 300 pm EDT.  The all time record is 31.60 feet set on December 10, 1919.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Paulding county Emergency Management personnel, local law enforcement, broadcast media, and newspapers reported that the historical and record flash flooding began to impact the southern portion of Paulding county during this time frame as repeated thunderstorms tracked across the area from the southwest.  The most significant flooding during this time period was associated with Sweetwater Creek, which began its climb to record flood stage.  By 1030 pm EDT, Sweetwater Creek near Brownsville and the Cobb/Paulding county line rose above flood stage.  Several roads, culverts, and some bridges were beginning to flood during this time frame.  Flood damage in southern Paulding county through 1100pm EST was estimated to be near $40,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low pressure area and associated cold front were moving from the mid-south into the southeast U.S.  A backdoor cold front was located across northeast Georgia.  A warm, tropical air mass was present between the advancing cold front to the west and the backdoor front across northeast Georgia.  Upper-level forcing was strong, thanks to the deep upper low moving into the southeast U.S.  These systems combined to bring a 24-hour period of heavy rain and thunderstorms that progressed from northwest to southeast across north and central Georgia.  With this event falling only 6 days after the record and catastrophic flooding that affected west central and north central Georgia on the 20th and 21st, flash flooding was a major concern.  Fortunately, the activity moved through more quickly, but additional flash flooding and river flooding was observed.  In addition, as the afternoon progressed and the activity moved into the central and east central portions of the state, an increasingly warm and unstable air mass was encountered, resulting in several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Citizen of Dalton reported that lightning struck and set the Rock Building on fire.  This was the site of the first high school in Murray County.  The building was completely destroyed from the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep full latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the system. During the afternoon of the 7th, strong northwest winds spread across the region behind the cold front, blowing down several trees in north Georgia, injuring one person in Whitfield county in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockmart Journal and the Cedartown Standard documented two flooding events in Polk county.  Euharlee Creek overflowed its banks in downtown Rockmart submerging a portion of Silver Comet Trail.  A portion of Seaborn James Park was also flooded.  Cedar Creek also overflowed its banks causing temporary flooding of some city streets in Cedartown.  A portion of West Avenue was briefly submerged under several inches of water at the height of the flooding.  Damage was limited to debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area.  Eleven deaths were recorded during the event, mostly vehicle related deaths at night from washed out bridges and roads as a result of swollen creeks.  Property damages are estimated to be at least $0.5 Billion, with hundreds of homes and businesses destroyed by flood waters.  Bridges on several state and local highways were washed out and some will take months to even a year to replace. Hundreds of homes, apartments, businesses, and roads sustained damages from flooding.  A number of culverts and/or bridges were washed out and several roads were closed. Monetary damage from flooding continued to mount, with damages exceeding $1.25 million during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historical, record, and catastrophic flood event unfolded during this period, mostly in the west central Georgia area, including the western and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta.  Major flooding was noted in many other areas of north and central Georgia, including the eastern suburbs of Atlanta, northwest Georgia, and parts of central Georgia.  The culprit was a very stagnant upper atmospheric pattern featuring a weak upper low that developed in early September across south Texas and slowly migrated east-northeast through September 22nd, until a more significant upper trough dropping south into the southern plains finally moved the pesky upper low northeast of Georgia.  In addition, an unusually deep tropical flow was noted throughout this period.  Precipitable water values exceeded 2.0 inches across the area during much of this period, resulting in extremely efficient rain producing cells.  Persistent heavy rain showers and thunderstorms began to plague the area on the 16th, and persisted daily through the 21st across parts of north and central Georgia.||However, the catastrophic flooding unfolded when a sea breeze moving northwest merged with existing outflow boundaries and higher terrain across the western suburbs of Atlanta to result in persistent heavy thunderstorms for a period of at least 12 hours that trained across the same counties west of Atlanta, generally affecting Carroll, Douglas, Paulding and Cobb counties.  As the activity progressed across the state, significant flooding unfolded further east, including Fulton county, and the city of Atlanta, DeKalb, and Gwinnett counties.  Incredible rainfall amounts of 15 to over 20 inches, were observed from northeast Carroll, through much of Douglas, into eastern Paulding and southern Cobb counties.  Rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were noted across parts of Fulton, Dekalb, and Gwinnett.  Rainfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were noted in other more spotty areas of northwest, north central, and central Georgia during this period.||The excessive rainfall on top of saturated ground resulted in some of the worst flooding ever reported in the Atlanta and north Georgia area.  Eleven deaths were recorded during the event, mostly vehicle related deaths at night from washed out bridges and roads as a result of swollen creeks.  Property damages are estimated to be at least $0.5 Billion, with hundreds of homes and businesses destroyed by flood waters.  Bridges on several state and local highways were washed out and some will take months to even a year to replace.   Eighteen counties (Bartow, Carroll, Chattooga, Cherokee, Cobb, Coweta, DeKalb, Douglas, Fulton, Gwinnett, Heard, Newton, Paulding, Rockdale, Stephens, and Walker) received a presidential disaster declaration as a result of the historic floods.  Twenty-one counties were declared eligible for public assistance, including several central Georgia counties that were affected by flash flooding earlier in the period.||Some of the worst flooding was observed along Sweetwater Creek near the Cobb, Fulton, Douglas county line.  Hundreds of homes were flooded and destroyed as the waters of Sweetwater Creek rose more than 20 feet above flood level.||Many main stem and secondary creeks, streams, and rivers across north and central Georgia, particularly in northwest and west central Georgia, reached record flood levels.  Many of these same creeks, streams, and rivers remained in flood for several days.  In some cases, all time record flood levels were recorded.  The following is a list of new record flood levels set on several creeks, streams, and rivers in north and west Georgia.  Noonday Creek near Woodstock rose to 19.6 feet on the 21st at 530 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.30 feet set on July 11, 2005.  Nickajack Creek at Mableton reached 19.30 feet on the 22nd at 215 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.60 feet set on July 11, 2005.  The North Fork of Peachtree Creek at Atlanta rose to 18.07 feet on the 21st at 715 pm EDT.  The previous record was 17.70 feet set on September 16, 2004.  Utoy Creek near Atlanta rose to 27.04 feet on the 22nd at 1000 am EDT.  The previous record was 16.86 feet set on May 6, 2003.  The Chattahoochee River at Whitesburg rose to 29.61 feet on the 21st at 745 pm EDT.  The previous record was 29.11 feet on December 11, 1919.  Suwanee Creek at Suwanee rose to 14.30 feet on the 21st at 645 pm EDT.  The previous record was 12.04 feet set on October 5, 1996.  The Yellow River at Lithonia rose to 25.50 feet on the 22nd at 515 am EDT.  The previous record was 17.53 feet set on May 7, 2003.  The Yellow River near Conyers below Milstead rose to 22.54 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 16.36 feet set on July 8, 2005.  The Chattahoochee River at Franklin rose to 29.98 feet on the 22nd at 500 pm EDT.  The previous record was 28.40 feet set on December 15, 1919.  The Sweetwater Creek at Austell rose to 30.17 feet on the 22nd at 1000 pm EDT.  The previous record was 21.81 feet on July 12, 2005.||The following rivers reached flood levels that were within the top five all time levels at these locations.  Peachtree Creek in Atlanta rose to 23.89 feet on the 21st at 915 pm EDT.  The all time record is 25.80 feet recorded on December 20th, 1919.  Nancy Creek in Atlanta reached 14.69 feet on the 21st at 930 pm EDT.  The all time record is 15.50 feet on December 1, 1973.  The Chattahoochee River at Vinings reached 28.10 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1919.  The South Fork of the Peachtree Creek reached 15.21 feet on the 22nd at 1200 am EDT.  The all time record is 29.0 feet set on December 10, 1976.  The Chattahoochee River at Campbellton reached 30.55 feet on the 22nd at 300 pm EDT.  The all time record is 31.60 feet set on December 10, 1919.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton County 911 Center reported that several roads were flooded throughout the county, especially the northern sections near the Atlanta Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport, Jonesboro, Riverdale, and Forest Park.  However, no roads were reported to have been closed during this time frame.  Some homes and apartments suffered minor flooding, especially in the Riverdale area.  Monetary damage was estimated to be $50,000.
