EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City confirmed that yet a second tornado touched down in Glascock county during the evening of April 10th.  This tornado was determined to be an EF1 and touched down in the far eastern part of the county, approximately 4.5 miles east-northeast of Gibson.  The tornado continued on an almost due eastward path for five miles into northern Jefferson county, lifting approximately three miles northeast of Stapleton.  The maximum path width was determined to be 200 yards with maximum winds estimated at 110 mph.  Damage within Glascock county from this tornado was confined to several downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although drought conditions were dramatically improving across the state from generous winter and early spring rainfall, several wildfires were reported by the Georgia Forestry Commission, mostly across north Georgia.  All of the wildfires in the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were human caused.||Wild fires were reported in Chattooga, Murray, Putnam, and Walker counties.  There were actually three seperate wild fire incidents in Murray county during the month.  Total combined damages from these wild fires was estimated to be nearly $22,000.  The most significant wildfire to occur during the month was in Walker county on March 22nd, burning 6.4 acres and causing $10,000 in damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire, labeled Town Mountain, burned 1.5 acres.  The fire was human caused.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Putnam County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees and power lines were down around Eatonton.  Trees were down on at least three mobile homes causing damage to the structures.  Radar data suggests that this storm traveled from just west of Eatonton to the southeast corner of the county near Flat Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north Florida and Southeast with a series of disturbances riding eastward along the boundary. Gulf moisture fed the storms with heavy Rainfall occurring across most of the region. On Thursday a strong disturbance over the Rockies became a low pressure system that finally drove a cold Front through the area on Thursday night. Jet dynamics were favorable for strong to severe storms during the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 08:47 a.m. EST the Echols County Sheriff reported 25 homes evacuated near Bryner Road, which leads to the river. Valentine road is closed due to a washout. This flooding was located in and around the town of Mayday where flooding was also occurring along the Alapaha River.  At 10:40 a.m. EST the public reported major flooding on both banks of the Alapaha River.  A house normally 10 feet above ground level had a water level 20 inches below the floor at that time. The rate of rise had decreased significantly that morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved across the CSRA and produced large tornadoes.  One tornado tracked across Columbia and Richmond counties then went into Aiken county South Carolina along highway 278.  The second tornado tracked across Burke county then into lower Barnwell and Allendale counties in South Carolina. Several homes were destroyed and many had moderate to severe damage.  Widespread trees and powerlines were also down.  Total damage estimate was 3 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McDuffie County paper reported a couple of trees down and a mobile home moved off the foundation by several feet on May road.

No tornado touch downs were reported or confirmed in this area, but Doppler radar clearly showed several mesoscale circulations passing through the county during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of half-dollar to golf ball-sized hail were received from the public in the northern part of Walker county near Chickamauga.  Penny-sized hail was reported from the southern part of the county near LaFayette.  Radar data supports that hail of similar size likely fell in a large swath from northwest to southeast across the county from High Point to Villanow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail in northern Douglas county, north of Douglasville near the Douglas/Paulding county line, penny-sized hail in Douglasville, and golf ball-sized hail in the southeast part of the county near and southeast of Chapel Hill.  The public reported that hail completely covered the ground in some areas.  Significant damage to vehicles was reported from this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast winds of 50 to 60 mph developed on the back side of a departing large complex of showers and thunderstorms as it moved southeast from northwest into central Georgia.  Widespread damage to trees, power lines, and several structures was observed across many north Georgia counties, especially in northwest and north central Georgia, during the early to mid-morning hours.  Most of the damage occurred in northwest and north central counties north of Interstate-20 and west of Interstate-85.  Below is a summary of counties reporting wind damage along with estimated wind speeds.||Bartow County (High Wind) - The Bartow County 911 Center reported that at least 25 downed trees and a number of power lines were reported to be down throughout the county.  At least 4000 residents of the county were left without power.  ||Carroll County (High Wind) - The Carroll County 911 Center reported that many trees and power lines, at least 35, were blown down throughout the county.  Widespread power outages were observed.||Cherokee County (High Wind) - The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that more than 100 trees were down across the county.  At least 40 roads were blocked from downed trees.  Several homes sustained structural damage, at least 12 of which suffered moderate damage.  At least 10,000 residents of the county were left without power following the event.||Cobb County (Strong Wind) - The public reported that several trees were blown down near South Cobb Drive.||Clarke County (Strong Wind) - A storm spotter reported that a large Oak tree was blown down across University Drive.  A number of large tree limbs were down along Millege Avenue.||Coweta County (High Wind) - The public reported significant damage to structures in the northwestern part of the county.  A roof was blown off a barn causing the walls to collapse.  At least six homes between Summers McKoy Road and Tom Witcher Road sustained roof and siding damage from strong to high winds.  Several power outages were reported in the area from downed trees and power lines.||Dade County (High Wind) - A trained spotter estimated wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph.  Several trees down across the county.  Electric transformers exploding from downed trees and tree limbs on power lines.||Dawson County (High Wind) - The Dawson County Emergency Management Director reported that many trees were down in the Big Canoe area of northern Dawson county.  Seven homes were damaged during the wind storm, including one on Bearden Road that suffered significant damage.  A female was initially trapped in the home, but was able to escape on her own.  In addition, an individual was injured when they were struck by a tree limb.  Thirty-five roads were blocked and closed from widespread downed trees.  ||DeKalb County (High Wind) - The public reported that several trees and power lines were blown down in the northern part of the county, especially around Decatur.  Trees and/or power lines were down on Ashford-Dunwoody Road, Cross Street, Stratfield Drive N.E., Windsor Parkway N.E., and near Oglethorpe University.||Fannin County (Strong Wind) - The public reported that several trees and power lines were blown down in the eastern part of the county at elevations above 2000 feet.||Floyd County (High Wind) - The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that more than 80 trees were down across the county along with many power lines and utility poles.  Horse Leg Creek Road was blocked from utility lines.  Several homes also sustained minor to moderate damaged from downed trees, especially on East Third and Wright Streets in Rome.  At least 10,000 residents of the county were left without power following the event.||Forsyth County (High Wind) - The Forsyth County Emergency Management Director reported that many trees and power lines were blown down across the county.  At least four homes in the Cumming area sustained major damage was several large trees fell on the structures.  One of these homes was rendered uninhabitable until it could be repaired.  Blown electrical transformers started fires on some utility poles.  A vehicle was destroyed and a home suffered minor damage on Buford Drive when a large oak tree fell on the property.  Extensive damage was also reported at a marina at Port Royale on Lake Lanier.  The strong winds caused two docks to collide.  This action caused two boats to collide, one subsequently sunk, and a third was pushed over a walkway.||Fulton County (High Wind) - The public reported that several trees were down in the northern portion of the county.  A tree had fallen on a home at 125 West Spalding Drive in Sandy Springs causing extensive damage to the home.  An individual was killed on Nacoochee Drive near the Peachtree Battle Shopping Center in Buckhead, approximately five miles north of Atlanta, when a tree fell across the cab of his truck.  The Atlanta ASOS, 10-15 miles south of the area with the heaviest damage, recorded a wind gust of 45 knots at 953 am EDT.  Higher winds may well have been observed in the northern part of the county.||Gwinnett County (High Wind) - The Gwinnett Daily post reported that at least 30 trees were down across the county.  Wind gusts were estimated at 45 mph, but radar data suggest that stronger winds may have occurred in some areas.  A police officer in Suwanee was struck in the head by a falling tree and injured.||Hall County (High Wind) - The Hall County Emergency Management Director reported that many trees were down across the county, especially the south part of the county.  Several homes suffered extensive damage from downed trees.  Several boats were overturned on Lake Lanier at the Lan-Mar Marina.  A man was injured on Joe Chandler Road when a tree fell on him causing cuts and bruises to his head.  The Gainesville Times reported that nearly 300 trees had to be removed from roadways across the county.  Nearly 9000 residents of the county were left without power during the event as well.||Murray County (Strong Wind) - The RAWS station at Cohutta recorded a wind gust of 48 mph, almost high wind criteria.  A wind gust of 50 mph was reported from the Murray county mesonet site five miles east-southeast of Cisco.  Up to 10 trees were blown down in the northern part of the county.||Paulding County (High Wind) - The Paulding County Emergency Management Director reported extensive damage across Paulding county.  There were more than 50 incidents of downed trees and power lines.  There were 37 trees down on roads.  Four vehicles were either heavily damaged or destroyed from downed trees.  Eight structures were damaged, two from downed trees.  The roof was blown off a dry cleaners store and shingles were blown off the roof of two fire stations.  Vinyl siding was blown off a home on Crestworth Crossing Road in the far southeastern part of the county near Powder Springs.  The only weather observing station in Paulding county is a RAWS station in Dallas, which recorded 39 mph wind gusts.  However, the extent of the damage indicated that wind gusts of 58 mph were likely observed in some areas.||Pickens County (High Wind) - The public observed that many trees were down from Talking Rock to Jasper.||Union County (High Wind) - The Union County Emergency Management Director reported that many trees and power lines were down across the county.  A number of trees were down on power lines and widespread power outages were observed across the county.  The Brasstown Bald RAWS station recorded a wind gusts of 44 knots.  Damage suggested that higher wind gusts were likely observed in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cartersville Tribune reported that at least 25 downed trees and a number of power lines were down throughout the county.  At least 4000 residents of the county were left without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of penny, quarter, and golf ball-sized hail received from the Smyrna and Vinings area in eastern Cobb county.  This was the second of four severe thunderstorms to impact Cobb county during the evening of April 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail just south of Emerson and quarter-sized hail southeast of Cartersville.  Radar data suggests that similar-sized hail likely fell from Cartersville to the southeast corner of the county near Lake Allatoona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms on the 31st produced large hail, a brief weak tornado, and flash flooding in portions of southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported at Barnetts Creek and U.S. Highway 84.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper troughs were moving through a large, deep upper trough centered in the mid part of the U.S.  One such upper trough and associated cold front were sweeping through the southeast U.S. on April 1st, with another stronger system on the 2nd.  Widespread heavy rain was observed in central Georgia with the first system.  Several creeks and streams exceeded bankful and several roads and low-lying areas in central Georgia counties, especially around Macon, were flooded.  This evolved into a minor severe weather event during the early morning hours of April 2nd.  A more significant severe weather outbreak was observed when the main upper system and cold front swept into the southeast U.S. late on April 2nd and into the early morning hours of the 3rd.  A narrow line of thunderstorms, with a noteable lack of lightning, produced damaging winds gusts of at least 60 mph as it moved across the Floyd and Calhoun areas of northwest Georgia.  The line weakened considerably as it tracked further eastward into the state after midnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Management Director reported flooding to woodlands and adjacent fields and lowlands along Swain Creek in the eastern part of the county and the Little Ohopee River near Kite.  Power line road was washed out and closed.  Six other roads in the area had water flowing over the road.  In addition, three camp houses along the Oconee River on the western side of the county were threatened from high water.  The combination of saturated soil and windy conditions caused six trees to fall on homes near the Oconee River resulting in minor damage to the structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed half-dollar-sized hail southwest of Arcade while another storm spotter observed penny-sized hail in southern Jackson county, south of Jackson.  This was the second severe thunderstorm to track across the county, this time across the far southern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many reports of quarter sized-hail were received across northern Cobb county from Acworth to Kennesaw to Marietta and the northeast corner of the county.  The public reported golf ball-sized hail northeast of Acworth near the Cherokee county line.  The public estimated that wind gusts with the thunderstorm were near 50 mph, but no structural, tree, or power line damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of large hail were received from southern Cherokee county.  The public observed penny to quarter-sized hail from southwest Cherokee county approximately six miles west-northwest of Woodstock into the city of Woodstock.  Golf ball-sized hail was observed by the public in the southeast corner of the county, or about five miles east-southeast of Woodstock.  The Fulton County EMA Director was in the area at the time and estimated wind gusts of 60 mph.  However, no tree, power line, or structural damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bibb County Emergency Management Director reported that around 100 homes in the Macon area experienced basement flooding as a result of several days of heavy rainfall.  Parts of the county had a three-day rainfall total of five to six inches.  This rain fell on saturated soils from prior weeks of above normal rainfall.  In addition, up to 20 roads had to be closed as a result of flooding or ponding of water.  Minor flooding was also observed in fields and woodlands adjacent to creeks, streams, and rivers across the county.  Flood damages were generally the result of basement flooding, with only minor road repair or debris removal required.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail near Jasper and quarter-sized hail northeast of Nelson in the far southeast part of the county.  Nickel-sized hail was observed by the public in Marble Hill.  Radar data and previous reports from Gordon county support quarter or larger sized-hail beginning west of Blaine near the Gordon county line.  The public reported that hail fell for up to 20 minutes in the Marble Hill area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that golf ball-sized hail fell at Turner Field just southeast of Atlanta.  The public observed penny-sized hail on the north side of Atlanta.  This was the fourth severe thunderstorm to track across Fulton county during the evening of April 23rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail west of Dahlonega and penny to quarter-sized hail west of Georgia Highway 9 in the Mill Creek Station Subdivision near the Dawson county line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia, confirmed that an EF1 tornado had touched down approximately 2.5 miles south of Ochille and continued on an east-southeastward track across central Chattahoochee county, lifting about 4.0 miles east-southeast of its touchdown point.  The path width was determined to be 440 yards with maximum winds estimated at 80 mph.  The damage appeared to have begun as a downburst, within which the brief EF1 tornado spin up occurred.  Combined damage from the downburst and EF1 tornado consisted of destruction of several out buildings and sheds.  Hundreds of trees were flattened along the path of the tornado.  Another EF1 tornado was spawned from the same supercell thunderstorm, also within Chattahoochee county, just eight minutes later and four miles further east.

This fire was started as a result of power lines arcing in the wind.  The fire only consumed 1/2 acre.||Towns county - The Georgia Forestry commission reported a significant human-caused wildfire fueled by strong winds and low relative humidity in the mountains of Towns county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of large hail were received from the public across Clayton county as a slow moving severe thunderstorm tracked from the northwest to the east central part of the county.  The ASOS observer at the Atlanta Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport observed penny to quarter-sized hail off and on for a period of 46 minutes.  Golf ball-sized hail was reported from the public in Forest Park and Morrow, while penny to quarter-sized hail fell near Rex, east of Morrow, and northeast of Jonesboro as the storm began to weaken slightly as it approached the eastern border of the county.  In addition, the wind gusted to 42 knots at Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport during the hailstorm.  Frequent to continuous cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed at the airport during the thunderstorm.

The tornado was determined to have a maximum path width of 200 yards with maximum winds estimated to be 120 mph.  Considerable damage was noted in the Summerville area.  Over 30 homes were damaged along with 10 businesses.  Most of the damage to these structures was determined to be minor.  The majority of the damage occurred on Commerce Street.  The brick wall of a car care center collapsed, the roof was shifted and lifted from a lumber warehouse, and windows were blown out of a barbecue restaurant.  All of these events occurred on Commerce Street.  At the intersection of Cleghorn and Scoggin Streets nearby, a single-wide mobile home was completely destroyed.  Although the mobile home was anchored, the metal frame was ripped from the foundation and the home was tossed on its side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low was moving from the southern plains toward the Ohio Valley.  A strong Pacific cold front accompanied the upper low.  A narrow line of severe thunderstorms tracked across central Georgia during the morning hours.  These thunderstorms produced wind damage in several central Georgia counties.  Behind this line of thunderstorms, a large complex of showers and thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia during the early morning hours.  Widespread damaging winds developed on the back side of this complex of showers and thunderstorms across north Georgia.  Winds gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed along with widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures across many north Georgia counties, especially in northwest and north central Georgia, during the early to mid-morning hours.||Another round of thunderstorms developed during the late night and early morning hours on the 14th as the main upper low tracked through the southeast and southern Appalachian area.  A few of these storms became marginally severe across east central and southeast Georgia during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter Electric Management Cooperative (EMC) reported that several trees and tree limbs were down across the county.  This brought some power lines down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved into extreme northeast Georgia in the early evening hours. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado damage path began near Farmers Academy Rd, about 0.5 mile south of highway 106. The tornado crossed Red Hill School Rd, where some outbuildings were destroyed and a couple of mobile homes received minor roof damage. As the tornado continued northeast, some frame homes on highway 106 received minor roof damage. The most significant damage occurred on Crumps Bridge Rd, where one large chicken house was completely destroyed, and several others received major damage. The tornado continued to move northeast, uprooting or snapping off numerous trees as it crossed highway 145 and Greater Hope Rd. From there, the tornado began to move in a more east/northeast direction, continuing to blow down trees as it moved roughly parallel to North Clarks Creek Rd.  As the tornado crossed Hulsey Rd, roofs were blown off the wood frame addition to a mobile home and a barn. After the tornado crossed highway 17 and Pleasant Hill Circle, it continued to turn more to the right, traveling due east near the to Wilson Rd, where a mobile home was moved off its foundation, and the steel siding and some roofing material was blown off a building. The tornado continued to travel east, or even east/southeast, before lifting in the Gerrard Rd area.

Supercell thunderstorms moved into extreme northeast Georgia in the early evening hours. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage.
A warm front lifted northward into southeast Georgia spawning the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team determined a tornado occurred south of the Credit Hill community off Briardam Road around 440 PM Eastern Daylight Time, which is located about 3 miles southeast of Townsend. The tornado traveled to the northeast across an open field and produced extensive tree damage to various pines and hardwoods. The tornado crossed Briardam Road and struck the First Credit Hill Baptist Church, lifting and destroying its large front end porch. Both the tree and structural damage to the church were consistent with an EF-1 tornado on the Enhanced Fujita Scale with winds estimated to be around 100 miles per hour. The tornado continued to move rapidly northeast while skirting the ground in various locations. The tornado crossed highway 57 and produced tree and fence damage to several areas along Church of God Road, before crossing interstate 95 around mile marker 61|where additional tree damage occurred. The tornado appears to have lifted around 457 PM eastern daylight time shortly before reaching highway 17 near the intersection with Tramm Road. The total track length of the tornado was 8 miles with a maximum width of 770 yards.|No fatalities or injuries were reported with this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that a brief EF0 tornado touched down in western Sumter county near the Experimental Station on U.S. Highway 280, approximately one mile northeast of Plains, and tracked about one mile to the east-southeast before lifting.  The tornado was determined to have a maximum path width of 200 yards with maximum winds estimated at 100 mph.  Wind equipment at the Plains Research Center measured a wind gust of 110 mph as the tornado passed nearby.  This was the second of three tornadoes to affect Sumter county during the late evening of April 10th and the early morning hours of April 11th.   Damage along the tornado path was spotty with many downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another deep upper trough was translating across the south central and southeast United States accompanied by a strong Pacific cold front.  Although instability was marginal for the time of year, the upper atmosphere was highly sheared with a strong low-level jet.  Showers and a few thunderstorms moved across the area during the day, but it was during the late evening hours when the front moved into the area from the west that strong to severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Alabama and moved into western Georgia.  The activity took on the form of a squall line with distinct bowing segments.  Two EF1 tornadoes were spawned within this line of thunderstorms causing considerable damage to homes and businesses in the Columbus and Woodstock areas.  The activity quickly dissipated shortly after midnight as it moved further east into central and northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker County Emergency Management Director reported that a few trees were blown down in the southern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front pushed through the southeast where kinematic profiles and anomalously cold air aloft existed. This lead to the development of strong storms over portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A emergency manager reported extensive tree damage, downed power lines and several damaged porches along Tuckasee King Road, King Boulevard and Morgan Cemetery Road about 3 miles north of Clyo, Georgia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that penny to golf ball-sized hail fell across far northern Fulton county around Alpharetta.  The public observed penny-sized hail near Crabapple.  Most of the hail reports were penny to nickel-sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep negatively titled trough was rotating through the southeast U.S. accompanied by a unusually strong polar cold front for late April.  While surface moisture was limited across the area as it had been removed from the area by the early week system that brought severe weather and tornadoes to the area, strong upper dynamics caused an area of showers to develop that moved across west central and central Georgia during the late afternoon.  As these showers moved across Houston county, strong to high winds were observed.  Winds estimated at 45 to 55 mph and possibly greater blew down a number of large trees across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees were blown down across the county by high winds associated with a line of showers moving through the area which accompanied a strong cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barrow County 911 Center reported golf ball-sized hail in the Winder area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another deep upper trough was translating across the south central and southeast United States accompanied by a strong Pacific cold front.  Although instability was marginal for the time of year, the upper atmosphere was highly sheared with a strong low-level jet.  Showers and a few thunderstorms moved across the area during the day, but it was during the late evening hours when the front moved into the area from the west that strong to severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Alabama and moved into western Georgia.  The activity took on the form of a squall line with distinct bowing segments.  Two EF1 tornadoes were spawned within this line of thunderstorms causing considerable damage to homes and businesses in the Columbus and Woodstock areas.  The activity quickly dissipated shortly after midnight as it moved further east into central and northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that an EF1 tornado, which initially touched down in Phenix City - Russell County, Alabama as an EF2 tornado at the intersection of Dillingham and Broad Streets, continued into Muscogee county, Georgia and the city of Columbus.  The total tornado path was determined to be approximately four miles in length, two of which fell within Muscogee county, Georgia.  The maximum path width was determined to be 250 yards with maximum winds estimated at 105 mph.  Damage within Georgia was first observed at Columbus State University.  Significant damage was observed to buildings and trees on the campus.  The Old Rankin Building, an older building which had been converted to dormitories, suffered substantial damage.  From this point, spotty tree, roof, and window damage was observed along the path of the tornado and it continued moving eastward.  At the intersection of 13th Street and 13th Avenue, a small utility truck used at a nursery had been picked up and moved at least 25 feet and through a neighboring fence.  Minor damage to displays, trees, and plants was also observed in the adjacent nursery.  From here to where the tornado lifted near the intersection of Macon Road and Preston Drive in Columbus, hundreds of large mature oak trees were either snapped or uprooted through Lake Bottom Park and Hilton Avenue and Harding Drive.  Extensive damage was observed to homes throughout this area, largely a result of the downed trees.  Many power lines were down along the path of the tornado as well.  Over 100 structures, including homes and businesses, suffered minor to moderate damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight on the 11th, a supercell thunderstorm spawned a tornado just northwest of Parrott and caused wind damage near Lee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst downed many trees and damaged several storage sheds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late afternoon severe thunderstorm on the 20th dropped large hail near Valdosta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported on Interstate 75 near Mile Marker 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 5th produced large hail, wind damage and a funnel cloud across portions of southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in southern Sylvester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 28th produced several reports of wind damage across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were down near downtown Albany.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms on the 13th produced wind damage, hail, flash flooding and a tornado across parts of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about two miles southwest of Fitzgerald near the municipal airport.  It destroyed a barn and blew a batting cage across a field.  It also uprooted or snapped numerous trees and blew down signs.  The tornado continued on a northeast path into portions of downtown Fitzgerald.  The brick facade of a building collapsed.  Much of the damage was caused by fallen trees.  The tornado was classified an EF1 with winds estimated between 80 and 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front continued to drift slowly southward through the state and had finally drifted into central Georgia by May 10th.  Weak disturbances embedded in west to southwest flow aloft within a mean eastern U.S. trough combined with a moist, unstable surface air mass, provided the ingredients necessary for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these storms became severe, producing damaging downburst winds and penny to quarter-sized hail.  The location of the front limited the severe weather to central areas of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pulaski County Emergency Management Director reported extensive wind damage in the northern part of the county from Hawkinsville to just northwest of Mobley Crossing near the Bleckley county line.  More than two dozen trees were down in the area and a number of power lines.  At least one structure suffered substantial damage when a tree fell on the structure.  Several homes on the northwest side of Hawkinsville, especially along Jackson and Dooly streets, sustained minor to moderate roof damage when shingles or portions of the roofs were torn off the homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front continued to drift slowly southward through the state and had finally drifted into central Georgia by May 10th.  Weak disturbances embedded in west to southwest flow aloft within a mean eastern U.S. trough combined with a moist, unstable surface air mass, provided the ingredients necessary for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County 911 Center reported that an apparent downburst had resulted in several dozen downed trees, power lines, and structural damage in the Centerville area.  Much of the damage was concentrated in the Sentry Oaks Neighborhood, where more than a dozen trees were down, some resulting in damage to homes.  A portion of Interstate-75 had to be closed until downed trees blocking the lanes could be cleared.  In addition, a business in Centerville, adjacent to the Galleria Mall, sustained moderate structural damage, including several blown out windows as well as damage to siding and awnings.  A number of trees were also blown down further east near and east of Warner Robins toward the Twiggs county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southern stream short wave was moving from the southern plains toward the southeast.  A cold front remained nearly stationary across the Tennessee Valley and into the Southern Plains.  A long-lived squall line of severe thunderstorms tracked from the mid-south during the morning hours into the southeast by late afternoon, reaching the Georgia border around 5 pm.  The line of thunderstorms weakened quickly as it progressed further eastward into the state, but not before blowing down several hundred trees and power lines, especially in parts of west central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cobb County 911 Center reported that more than a dozen trees were down across the county, including one that fell on a home causing damage to the structure.  A tree was also down on Alabama Road in Mareitta.  Radar data indicated that there were two lines of thunderstorms causing strong to damaging winds across Cobb county.  One line moved from near Powder Springs at 610 pm EDT to the northeast corner of the county, northeast of Marietta, by 630 pm EDT.  Another smaller line moved from Powder Springs at 630 pm EDT to near Smyrna around 645 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front in combination with the sea breeze and an unstable air mass, resulted in numerous severe thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported quarter size hail lasting for around 10 minutes in Ludowici, Georgia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette County 911 Center reported that at least 20 trees were down across the county.  The majority of the downed trees were in the western part of the county in the Peachtree City area.  An off duty National Weather Service employee observed that four large pine trees were snapped along Georgia Highway 74 near the Cooper Lighting Facility about three miles south of Peachtree City.  Amateur radio operators reported that a one foot-diameter tree was down in Fayetteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The subtropical upper low that had drifted from the eastern Gulf into the Arklatex region during the previous week had been absorbed into the mean flow and a broad upper trough.  The entire system was being pushed back east as an upper-level ridge was building across the western United States.  A cold front accompanied the upper trough.  With a warm, moist atmosphere remaining in place across north and central Georgia, yet another day of mostly pulse, afternoon and evening thunderstorms was observed.  Once again, a few of these became strong, with an isolated storm in Fannin county briefly becoming marginally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CocoRaHS observed reported nickel-sized hail in far southern Fannin county between Dial and Pisgah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 28th produced several reports of wind damage across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down to the east of Nashville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 28th produced several reports of wind damage across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were down about seven miles south of Bainbridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bleckley County Emergency Management Director and Macon television stations reported extensive wind damage in the southern part of Bleckley county.  Several dozen trees trees and many power lines were down, especially in the vicinity of Mac Thompson Road and Chicken Road.  Structural damage was also observed.  Some 20 to 25 homes sustained minor damage from downed trees.  One mobile home sustained significant damage when a tree fell on the structure.  A horse barn near the intersection of Mac Thomsron Road and Chicken Road collapsed during the strong winds.  A horse inside was initially rescued from the fallen structure, but later died from injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Macon television stations reported that from 20 to 30 trees were blown down or uprooted in the southern part of the county, especially within the Longleaf Subdivision near Elko.  This damage occurred from a separate thunderstorm from the one that was almost simultaneously causing extensive wind damage and penny to quarter-sized hail in the far northern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was slowly moving from the central U.S. toward the eastern U.S.  An accompanying cold front and Canadian high pressure system were moving southeast from the central U.S.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during afternoon heating in an increasing moist and unstable atmosphere.  On this particular day, a well defined sea breeze front was observed to move well inland from southeast Georgia during the early and mid-afternoon into northwest Georgia by evening.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms accompanied the sea breeze front as it moved northwest across the state.  A few of these became severe producing penny to quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed nickel to quarter-sized hail in the Villa Rica area of far northeast Carroll county from 825 to 835 pm EDT, and further west near Temple in the far northwestern part of the county from 855 pm to 858 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight on the 11th, a supercell thunderstorm spawned a tornado just northwest of Parrott and caused wind damage near Lee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down just west of County Line Road.  It raced eastward across the road and lifted just before crossing State Road 520.  Along its brief path, several mobile homes and barns were destroyed, a few frame homes were damaged, and many trees and power lines were down.  A woman escaped injury when her mobile home was blown off its foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper atmospheric and surface pattern remained virtually unchanged across the southeastern U.S.  A stationary front remained from the eastern Tennessee Valley into the Southern Plains.  A negatively tilted upper trough persisted across the mid-south and southeast U.S.  A warm, moist, unstable environment persisted across north and central Georgia.  Saturated ground and additional rainfall led to stream, creek, and river flooding in parts of north and central Georgia.  A few thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging downburst winds and marginal severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail in Thomaston and on the south side of Thomaston at the Raintree Golf Course.

A few homes in the area sustained minor to moderate damage from the downed trees.  A tree was also down on the northeast side of Atlanta at Glen Arden Way and North Highland Avenue.

Minor flooding of low lands adjacent to these creeks and streams occurred.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pattern of early May continued, namely a series of weak short waves moving through a broad trough across the eastern half of the U.S. and a stationary front draped from the Tennessee Valley southwest into Texas.  The majority of the thunderstorm development on this day was across north central and northeast Georgia.  A few storms became strong to severe during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported nickel-sized hail near Oasis in northwest Fannin county.

The 911 Center reported that some trees were down in many areas of the county.  A few trees were down on Georgia Highway 137 southwest of Roberta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed over Burke county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police dept. reported a few trees down on hwy 56.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of severe thunderstorms on the 28th affected southwest Georgia with numerous reports of wind damage, large hail and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down across the center part of the county, including Moultrie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of severe thunderstorms on the 28th affected southwest Georgia with numerous reports of wind damage, large hail and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water covered several roads between Grand Island Drive and North Slappey Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of severe thunderstorms on the 28th affected southwest Georgia with numerous reports of wind damage, large hail and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets in the central and southern portion of Sylvester were flooded.  Residents of several homes surrounded by high water were evacuated.  County emergency management officials were evacuated when flood waters surrounded their building on East Willingham Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of severe thunderstorms on the 28th affected southwest Georgia with numerous reports of wind damage, large hail and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was lifted off a large building near Dothan Road and Cyrene Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough and associated cold front continued to move slowly east into the Tennessee Valley and southeast U.S.  The cold front had moved to near Athens and Atlanta by the morning of May 17th.  Scattered thunderstorms continued to develop in the warm, moist air mass ahead of the front.  Some of these storms became strong to severe during the afternoon and the cold front continued to move southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail just southwest of Workmore.  Radar data suggests that large hail likely continued to the southeast side of Workmore.

The public observed penny to quarter-sized hail along the Talbot/Harris county line, approximately eight miles west of Talbotton.  A report of penny-sized hail was also received from the Talbot County 911 Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous subtropical upper low evolved across the eastern Gulf during the May 20th through May 23rd period, then drifted northwest into the mid-south and Arklatex region by the 25th.  A steady stream of Gulf moisture was transported across the area from the southeast on the east side of the northwestward moving low.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms accompanied this system for several days.  Because the atmosphere was overall rather stable, only a few storms became strong.  Isolated storms became marginally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed nickel-sized hail west of Thomaston in the western and northwest part of Upson county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtropical low continued to drift northwest through the Arklatex region and weaken.  A moist, tropical flow continued from southeast to northwest across the eastern Gulf into the subtropical low.  The pattern of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms, which began on May 21st, continued across north and central Georgia.  Each afternoon, southeast to northwest oriented bands of showers and thunderstorms organized and moved from central Georgia into north Georgia.  Most of the storms were non-severe, as on previous days.  However, as was the case on the 24th, at least one storm in west central Georgia became marginally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Troup County 911 Center reported penny to nickel-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front stalled over southeast Georgia during the afternoon hours. A seabreeze pushed inland and met the stalled boundary during the late afternoon and evening hours producing strong thunderstorms in an unstable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down on Poor Robin Road 9 miles east southeast of Sylvania, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and short wave trough were approaching Georgia from the northwest. A weakening mesoscale convective system and associated bow echo moved into northwest Georgia during the evening of May 1st producing a few severe thunderstorms and one funnel cloud report near Rome.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that funnel clouds had been observed near the Rome High School.  Radar data showed a mesocyclone in the northeast part of the county between Rome and Shannon during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front was located across the Tennessee Valley westward into Texas.  A series of weak disturbances were traversing the area in a mean westerly flow aloft in the warm, humid air mass south of the front.  This led to scattered showers and thunderstorms across north and central Georgia for several days.  One non-severe thunderstorm moving through Atlanta and central Fulton county produced wind gusts that blew down a large tree causing structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Atlanta metropolitan television stations reported that a large tree was blown down during a thunderstorm on Virginia Circle, about two miles north-northeast of downtown Atlanta.  The tree fell across a power line and onto two cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southern stream short wave was moving from the southern plains toward the southeast.  A cold front remained nearly stationary across the Tennessee Valley and into the Southern Plains.  A long-lived squall line of severe thunderstorms tracked from the mid-south during the morning hours into the southeast by late afternoon, reaching the Georgia border around 5 pm.  The line of thunderstorms weakened quickly as it progressed further eastward into the state, but not before blowing down several hundred trees and power lines, especially in parts of west central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coweta County Emergency Management Director reported that more than 100 trees were down across the county.  More than a dozen power lines were also down.  The majority of the damage was observed across the western and northern part of the county from Handy to north of Madras to near Palmetto, along the Fulton county line.  One resident reported that a downburst had occurred in the Handy area where several dozen trees were down at a private residence.  Trees down included large pine and oak trees.  Some trees were also down in the eastern and southern parts of the county near Newnan, Sharpsburg, and Senoia.  No structural damage was reported.  Approximately 3000 residents of the county were left without power for a few hours following the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over the waters and a trough of low pressure inland led to an enhanced pressure gradient along the Georgia coastline, resulting in strong onshore flow and several rip currents along coastal Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tybee Island Ocean Rescue reported several rip currents that were 25 to 40 feet wide and going out 200 feet from shore along Tybee Beach Georgia between 16th and 17th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of hail, ranging in size from pennies to quarters, were received from the public across central and eastern Coweta county.  Quarter-sized hail was observed near Newnan and north of Newnan, while penny-sized hail was observed near Sharpsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtropical low continued to drift northwest through the Arklatex region and weaken.  A moist, tropical flow continued from southeast to northwest across the eastern Gulf into the subtropical low.  The pattern of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms, which began on May 21st, continued across north and central Georgia.  Each afternoon, southeast to northwest oriented bands of showers and thunderstorms organized and moved from central Georgia into north Georgia.  Most of the storms were non-severe, as on previous days.  However, as was the case on the 24th, at least one storm in west central Georgia became marginally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Troup County 911 Center reported that at least seven trees were blown down in the La Grange area.  The La Grange Daily news reported that one of the downed trees fell on an adjacent porch and two vehicles in the 300 block of Park Avenue.  Another large oak tree split and fell on a home in the 1200 block of Third Avenue, damaging the roof and totaling a Honda Civic.  On Houston Street, a tree was blown down onto a power line and an adjacent vehicle.  A few other power lines were also down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge, which had been centered over Alabama for the previous three days, was retrograding west as a deep upper low developed off the east coast.  Similar to the June 10th - June 18th period, unstable northwest flow evolved across the area once again.  The pattern allowed a backdoor cold front to push into the area from the northeast, further providing lift in an unstable air mass for thunderstorms.  Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across north central Georgia during the mid-afternoon and quickly moved southeast into central Georgia by evening.  The majority of the severe events occurred in central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cobb County 911 Center reported that at least 15 trees were down around Marietta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was located across the southeast and south central U.S.  A nearly stationary cold front extended from the Tennessee Valley into the mid-south.  A warm, humid, unstable air mass remained across the region.  Scattered to numerous showers and a few thunderstorms were noted across all of north and central Georgia.  Some brought heavy rainfall resulting in minor flooding and street flooding during the afternoon, especially in the Atlanta and Columbus metropolitan areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that heavy rain resulted in a small unnamed tributary, located just west of Interstate 85 and between Georgia Highway 166/Langford Parkway and Langston Drive, rising out of its banks and flooding an adjacent home.  The flooding to the home was confined to the yard, crawl space, garage, and a parked car.  Further examination determined that the flooding was the result of a collapsed culvert pipe casued  by heavy runoff and debris buildup in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large subtropical ridge across Texas was slowly expanding eastward.  In advance of this upper ridge, an unstable northwest flow aloft, with embedded short waves, prevailed across the eastern and southeastern U.S.  This, combined with a continuing warm and very unstable air mass, supported development of several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms across the region from late on the 17th through the 18th.  The first round developed around midnight on the 17th and continued through the early morning hours.  The second round occurred during the late afternoon on the 18th.  The storms were generally confined to the eastern parts of the state with all of the storms developing within narrow northwest to southeast oriented bands.  The individual storms were also tracking from northwest to southeast within these bands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Augusta television stations reported that several large trees were down on Georgia Highway 23 between Garfield and Twin City in the far eastern part of the county.

The storms were generally confined to the eastern parts of the state with all of the storms developing within narrow northwest to southeast oriented bands.  The individual storms were also tracking from northwest to southeast within these bands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge, which had been centered over Alabama for the previous three days, was retrograding west as a deep upper low developed off the east coast.  Similar to the June 10th - June 18th period, unstable northwest flow evolved across the area once again.  The pattern allowed a backdoor cold front to push into the area from the northeast, further providing lift in an unstable air mass for thunderstorms.  Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across north central Georgia during the mid-afternoon and quickly moved southeast into central Georgia by evening.  The majority of the severe events occurred in central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Butts County Emergency Management Director reported that an apparent thunderstorm downburst or microburst caused extensive damage to trees, power lines, and property in the northeastern part of the county from the Henry county line, just northwest of Fincherville to the south side of Lake Jackson along the Butts/Jasper county line.  At least 40 trees were down along this path.  Some of the most significant damage occurred near the intersection of Georgia Highway 36 and Barnetts Bridge Road, where a number of trees and power lines were down and one home in the area suffered major damage.  A total of six homes sustained damage, two of which were severely damaged and four others sustained moderate damage.

A few storms approached severe limits.

One of the trees fell on a house on Tunnel-Hill Varnell Road causing significant damage to the structure.  Some power lines and power poles were also down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance was tracking across the southern plains into the Tennessee valley around a large subtropical ridge in the western Gulf of Mexico.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the early and mid-afternoon across northwest and north central Georgia and then moved east-southeast across the northern half of the state.  A few storms became strong to severe producing marginally severe hail as well as frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitfield County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a home northeast of Dalton in the Pleasant Grove community.  The structure suffered at least minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through southeast Georgia within a highly unstable and moist environment. Strong pulse type thunderstorms developed over inland Georgia before shifting toward the Georgia coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on a car at a residence along Hall Road one mile north of Ludowici, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southern stream short wave was moving from the southern plains toward the southeast.  A cold front remained nearly stationary across the Tennessee Valley and into the Southern Plains.  A long-lived squall line of severe thunderstorms tracked from the mid-south during the morning hours into the southeast by late afternoon, reaching the Georgia border around 5 pm.  The line of thunderstorms weakened quickly as it progressed further eastward into the state, but not before blowing down several hundred trees and power lines, especially in parts of west central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll County Emergency Management Director reported that at least a dozen trees were down across the county.  One home in Amindon Estates suffered structural damage when a tree fell through the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 14th produced several reports of wind damage across portions of southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was slowly moving from the central U.S. toward the eastern U.S.  An accompanying cold front and Canadian high pressure system were moving southeast from the central U.S.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during afternoon heating in an increasing moist and unstable atmosphere.  On this particular day, a well defined sea breeze front was observed to move well inland from southeast Georgia during the early and mid-afternoon into northwest Georgia by evening.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms accompanied the sea breeze front as it moved northwest across the state.  A few of these became severe producing penny to quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed nickel to quarter-sized hail in far western Douglas county west of Winston and toward Villa Rica and the Carroll county border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An broad negatively-tilted upper trough continued to push through the southeastern U.S.  A cold front across the Tennessee Valley and into the mid-south was only making limited southeastward progress and was still located just northwest and west of Georgia.  This allowed scattered showers and thunderstorms to once again develop across mainly central Georgia.  A few of these thunderstorms approached severe limits.  In addition, a small wake low wind event was observed in west central Georgia around Fort Benning as the area of showers and thunderstorms departed to the east and south of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County Emergency Management Director reported that three trees were blown down near Cordelle.  One fell on a police vehicle while being driven, causing the car to hydroplane.  The car was totaled.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A southerly wind gust of 59 mph was measured at Lawson Air Field in the Fort Benning Military Reservation.  The wind was associated was likely the effect of a small wake low caused by outflow from a departing area of showers and thunderstorms to the east and south.  No tree, power line, or structural damage was observed in the generally highly wooded area of the military reservation.

Widespread power outages were observed within the county from downed power lines.  No structural damage was reported, however.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 14th produced several reports of wind damage across portions of southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were down.  Scattered power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and short wave trough were approaching Georgia from the northwest. A weakening mesoscale convective system and associated bow echo moved into northwest Georgia during the evening of May 1st producing a few severe thunderstorms and one funnel cloud report near Rome.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees were blown down by strong straight-line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper atmospheric and surface pattern remained virtually unchanged across the southeastern U.S.  A stationary front remained from the eastern Tennessee Valley into the Southern Plains.  A negatively tilted upper trough persisted across the mid-south and southeast U.S.  A warm, moist, unstable environment persisted across north and central Georgia.  Saturated ground and additional rainfall led to stream, creek, and river flooding in parts of north and central Georgia.  A few thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging downburst winds and marginal severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS gage on the Ogeechee River at Midville rose to the 6.0 foot flood stage during the evening of May 6th.  This caused backup of water and minor flooding on William Swamp Creek, Rocky Creek, and Daniels Creek, upstream from Ogeechee River at Midville.  Minor flooding of low lands adjacent to these creeks and streams occurred.  Damage was confined to minor debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough and associated cold front continued to move slowly east into the Tennessee Valley and southeast U.S.  The cold front had moved to near Athens and Atlanta by the morning of May 17th.  Scattered thunderstorms continued to develop in the warm, moist air mass ahead of the front.  Some of these storms became strong to severe during the afternoon and the cold front continued to move southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that at least five trees were down just southwest of Workmore.  Fence damage was damaged from the downed trees as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front continued to drift slowly southward through the state and had finally drifted into central Georgia by May 10th.  Weak disturbances embedded in west to southwest flow aloft within a mean eastern U.S. trough combined with a moist, unstable surface air mass, provided the ingredients necessary for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these storms became severe, producing damaging downburst winds and penny to quarter-sized hail.  The location of the front limited the severe weather to central areas of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to quarter-sized hail in far eastern Harris county, just east of Waverly Hall at the intersection of Georgia Highway 208 and 36, eastward toward the Talbot county line, continuing into Talbot county.  This hail occurred from a re-intensification of the the same thunderstorm that earlier dumped nickel-sized hail west of Mulberry Grove.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down across Lake Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper atmospheric and surface pattern across the southeastern U.S. persisted.  A negatively tilted upper trough persisted across the mid-south and southeast U.S.  The environment across the region was slightly more stable than on previous days as the more moist and unstable air had been pushed slightly further south into central Georgia.   Afternoon heating and abundant low-level moisture allowed scattered thunderstorms to form during the afternoon, with a few becoming marginally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail in extreme northeast Henry county, just north of Whitehouse near the intersection of Georgia Highway 138 and White Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late evening thunderstorms on the 2nd caused wind damage in and around Valdosta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on an apartment complex, which required the evacuation of several residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft persisted across the southeast United States as the result of a large subtropical high anchored over the southern Plains.  A cold front was approaching the area from the northwest.  The front, combined with disturbances in the northwest flow aloft, brought another round of showers and thunderstorms to the area during the maximum heating hours of afternoon and evening. While the thunderstorms remained below severe limits, frequent cloud-to-ground lightning and heavy rain were common in north Georgia.  Some lightning fires were reported, a few trees were blown down, and flash flooding was observed in parts of metropolitan Atlanta.  During the early morning hours of the 13th, a mesoscale convective complex moved southeast out of Tennessee into north central and west central Georgia.  While these thunderstorms were not severe, frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed once again, affecting one home shortly after midnight in Floyd county and another in Fayette county around 530 am EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rome News Tribune reported that several trees were blown down on Coral Avenue and Cave Spring Road on the southwest side of Rome, between Rome and Cave Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in the lee of the Appalachians in combination with the sea breeze pushing into an unstable air mass, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees and power lines down along Hodgeville Road, 5 miles south-southwest of Rincon, Georgia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bloomingdale, Georgia Fire Department reported quarter size hail at the fire station.

She was treated and released from the hospital with non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front was edging westward across Georgia as a deep closed upper low remained anchored off the mid-Atlantic coast.  The front served as a focusing mechanism for scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many of these storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  A few became severe and produced marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lamar County 911 Center observed penny-sized hail in the Barnesville area.

Several homes in the Virginia Highland area of far eastern Fulton county, near the DeKalb county line had flooded basements.  Minor flooding was also observed along Piedmont Road slightly further north.  The North Fork of Peachtree Creek rose above action stage, but did not reach flood stage during this event.

The home was completely destroyed by the resulting large fire.  The residents of the home were not home at the time of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front remained across central Georgia, with a moist easterly upslope flow evident north of the stalled front across north Georgia, especially in the northeast mountains. Meanwhile, as was the case most of the summer of 2009, northwest flow aloft remained in place across the southeast U.S. thanks to a mean, nearly stationary trough or upper low anchored across the northeast U.S.  Showers and thunderstorms developed early in the morning on the 8th across northeast Georgia and trained from south to north across the higher terrain for several hours through much of the morning.  The result was four to six inches of rain across central and south Towns county, with two to three inch amounts across eastern Union and far northern White counties.  Flash flooding was observed in southern Towns county from this event.||During the afternoon, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across central Georgia producing marginally severe hail and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Times Herald of Newnan reported that lightning struck a home on Tudor Way off Rockaway Road.  The resulting fire was contained mostly to the attic as it was quickly extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a small area of flash flooding over extreme Northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along South Fork Mud Creek caused water to pool to waist-depth on Hoyt St in Cornelia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a tree down on the road near the intersection of White Bluff Road and Coffee Bluff Road in the White Bluff area of Savannah, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front was draped across central Georgia, with a moist easterly upslope flow evident north of the stalled front across north Georgia. As was the case much of the summer of 2009, northwest flow aloft remained in place across the southeast U.S. thanks to a mean, nearly stationary trough or upper low anchored across the northeast U.S.  Numerous pre-existing convective outflow boundaries provided ample support for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and evening.  While none of the storms became severe, frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with most of the storms.  Two lightning-related fires occurred in the Atlanta metropolitan area during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The John's Creek Herald reported that lightning struck a home on Quail Brook in the John's Creek community, just southeast of Alpharetta.  No specific damage information was provided.
