EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual weather pattern was in place for July.  A fairly active west to northwest flow was evident across the southern United States with a broad upper low in place across the Great Lakes and New England.  A cold front extended from Texas into the Tennessee Valley and served as a focusing mechanism for shower and thunderstorm development in a warm, moist air mass present to the south of the front across Georgia and Alabama.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed and moved east-southeast across the area during the afternoon and evening hours.  A few of these storms became severe producing damaging wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front remained across central Georgia, with a moist easterly upslope flow evident north of the stalled front across north Georgia, especially in the northeast mountains. Meanwhile, as was the case most of the summer of 2009, northwest flow aloft remained in place across the southeast U.S. thanks to a mean, nearly stationary trough or upper low anchored across the northeast U.S.  Showers and thunderstorms developed early in the morning on the 8th across northeast Georgia and trained from south to north across the higher terrain for several hours through much of the morning.  The result was four to six inches of rain across central and south Towns county, with two to three inch amounts across eastern Union and far northern White counties.  Flash flooding was observed in southern Towns county from this event.||During the afternoon, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across central Georgia producing marginally severe hail and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Towns County Emergency Management Director reported that from four to six inches of rain fell in southern Towns county causing flash flooding just south of the community of Mountain Scene, or about eight miles south to southeast of Hiawassee.  The heavy rain forced High Shoals Creek, Soapstone Creek, and Corbin Creek, all headwaters of the Hiawassee River, to all exceed their banks and flood nearby areas.  A few private dirt roads were washed out by the flooded creeks.  No significant structural or property damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a trough of low pressure and the inland propagating sea breeze, resulted in the development of numerous thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 5 trees down and estimated winds of 60 to 65 mph as a thunderstorm moved through Herndon, Georgia.  A few shingles were also blown off the roof of a house by the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front was edging westward across Georgia as a deep closed upper low remained anchored off the mid-Atlantic coast.  The front served as a focusing mechanism for scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many of these storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  A few became severe and produced marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pike County 911 Center reported that a home on Fossett Road was struck by lightning.  No fire was reported and damage was minimal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walton County 911 Center relayed a report that two trees and one power line were down in the Loganville area near the Gwinnett county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep upper trough was noted across the eastern U.S., deepening southward from a parent upper low in the Great Lakes region.  This helped push an equally unusually strong cold front for mid-July southward through Georgia late on the 17th and into the 18th.  Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of the front.  While none of the storms reached severe limits, a few became strong.  One blew a tree down onto a vehicle in Atlanta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that a tree was blown down onto a car along Constitution Avenue, approximately two miles southeast of downtown Atlanta, near the DeKalb county line.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a stalled cold front across southern Georgia during the afternoon of the 2nd.  The storms produced numerous reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, including a large oak, were toppled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a stalled cold front across southern Georgia during the afternoon of the 2nd.  The storms produced numerous reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds toppled numerous trees and power lines from Nashville east to State Highway 135.  Two homes were set ablaze due to downed power lines.  Trees fell on several homes and vehicles in Nashville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a stalled cold front across southern Georgia during the afternoon of the 2nd.  The storms produced numerous reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few homes were damaged by fallen trees in Ray City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summer of northwest flow aloft continued with a strong subtropical ridge across the southwestern U.S. and a deep upper trough in the northeastern U.S.  A cold front was approaching north Georgia from the Tennessee valley.  Isolated thunderstorms developed across north Georgia during the afternoon and evening producing frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  None of the storms were observed to have become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post reported that lightning struck a two-story home on Castle Royal Drive.  The home sustained significant damage from the resulting fire.  A family of five was displaced as a result of this lightning-related fire.

Some minor structural damage was reported from downed trees.  Power was out for several hours to as many as 4000 people in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough was digging southward from the Great Lakes toward the southeast.  This was pushing a cold front toward Georgia from the northwest.  Southwest to northeast oriented bands of thunderstorms developed across mainly north central and northwest Georgia during the afternoon and evening.  These thunderstorms brought heavy rainfall of two to four inches in some north central and northwest Georgia counties.  One storm in Gilmer county produced damaging wind gusts.  Considerable cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with these thunderstorms, resulting in several house fires in the north Atlanta metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee Tribune reported that lightning struck a home on Eagle Drive in the Towne Lake area near Canton.  A fire resulted, beginning in the floor truss between the first and second floors of the home.  The fire was quickly extinguished.  The home suffered minor damage to the exterior walls.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable northwest flow aloft persisted across the area.  A cold front continued to slowly approach from the northwest.  However, a pre-frontal trough was located across northeast and north central Georgia.  This feature served as a focusing mechanism for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening, especially across east central and central Georgia.  A few of the thunderstorms became marginally severe and produced penny-sized hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail in the north central part of the county, near and just west of Norwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was moving south from the Tennessee Valley toward Georgia as an upper trough deepened across the eastern U.S.  A very moist, unstable atmosphere was in place across north Georgia in advance of the front.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and continued well into the evening.  A few of these thunderstorms became severe across northeast Georgia producing quarter-sized hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oconee County 911 Center reported that more than 15 trees and several power lines were down, especially across the eastern part of the county from just west of Bogart to near Barnett Shoals.  A large old oak tree fell on the roof of a home along North High Shoals Road resulting in moderate damage to the structure.  A mobile home on Dials Mill Spur was destroyed when a tree fell on it.  Trees were down on Mason Mill Road, Garrett Road, U.S. Highway 441, Fern Creek Drive, New High Shoals Drive, Jennings Mill Road, Monroe Highway, U.S. Highway 78 (Atlanta Highway) at several locations, and Pete Dickens Road.  An 18-wheel truck carrying gasoline wrecked when it ran into a tree that had fallen across Atlanta Highway near Pete Dickens Road.  The driver did not suffer any injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough, located across the eastern U.S., was helping to push a strong cold front into the southeast U.S.  Unseasonably cool air was noted behind the front. Many of these thunderstorms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning and a few became severe.  Several homes in the Atlanta area were struck by lightning and one person was injured from a lightning strike in Paulding county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Paulding County Emergency Management Director reported that at least nine homes across the county were struck by lightning.  Two of these lightning strikes resulted in fires and damage to the structures.  In addition, one individual approximately three miles north-northeast of New Georgia was injured as a result of a lightning strike.  Lightning also struck a power line approximately three miles north-northeast of New Georgia in the southern part of the county, causing the power line to fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north Florida and Southeast with a series of disturbances riding eastward along the boundary. Gulf moisture fed the storms with heavy Rainfall occurring across most of the region. On Thursday a strong disturbance over the Rockies became a low pressure system that finally drove a cold Front through the area on Thursday night. Jet dynamics were favorable for strong to severe storms during the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Satilla River entered flood on April 1st 2009 due to heavy rainfall over the preceding two weeks, but more specifically extreme rainfall over the prior 35 hours. Several small communities with mobile homes were flooded with an unknown damage amount.  The river crested in Waycross on April 5th approximately 15:30 EST with crest of 22.4 feet, flood stage being 16 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse quasi-stationary frontal boundary extended from the South Carolina coast to southwest Georgia. In general isolated convection developed along the front, however, more steady convection focused on coastal Georgia. A strong northeast low level flow from the Atlantic Ocean led to speed convergence over the coastal waters with a mid level return southwesterly flow. In the upper levels several impulses moved across the areas with upper level difluence supporting localized convection. Convection developed over the Georgia Coastal waters and worked its way west toward the lower Georgia coastal areas. This convection focused over portions of eastern Glynn County and with strong mid to upper level support dropped between 6 and 10 inches of rain over portions of the City of Brunswick, Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glynn County, Georgia Emergency Management reported that Chapel Crossing Road had to be closed during the afternoon of the 1st due to high water. Subsequently numerous streets were closed in the City of Brunswick due to closed basin flooding. Closed streets included Chapel Crossing Road, Fairway Oaks Drive, Belle Point Parkway, Fairman Drive, Glynnmarsh Drive, Enterprise Street, Hornet Drive, Shangri-La Avenue and Baybridge Drive. Other flooded areas included Kemble Avenue, Heron Street, N. Cleburn street, Parkwood Street, Wildwood Drive, King Cotton Road, Old Mission road, Key Circle, and Community Road. Several homes in the College Park neighborhood of Brunswick had to be evacuated and had water 6 inches deep inside. The flooding of homes resulted from several days of heavy rainfall. A shelter was opened at Seldon Park on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large subtropical ridge across Texas was slowly expanding eastward.  In advance of this upper ridge, an unstable northwest flow aloft, with embedded short waves, prevailed across the eastern and southeastern U.S.  This, combined with a continuing warm and very unstable air mass, supported development of several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms across the region from late on the 17th through the 18th.  The first round developed around midnight on the 17th and continued through the early morning hours.  The second round occurred during the late afternoon on the 18th.  The storms were generally confined to the eastern parts of the state with all of the storms developing within narrow northwest to southeast oriented bands.  The individual storms were also tracking from northwest to southeast within these bands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Management Director reported that a home was struck by lightning just north of Wrightsville causing damage to the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms on the 18th produced reports of large hail, lightning and damaging winds across portions of south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported just north of the Valdosta Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms on the 18th produced reports of large hail, lightning and damaging winds across portions of south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms on the 18th produced reports of large hail, lightning and damaging winds across portions of south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines down with scattered power outages west and southwest of Alapaha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse quasi-stationary frontal boundary extended from the South Carolina coast to southwest Georgia. In general isolated convection developed along the front, however, more steady convection focused on coastal Georgia. A strong northeast low level flow from the Atlantic Ocean led to speed convergence over the coastal waters with a mid level return southwesterly flow. In the upper levels several impulses moved across the areas with upper level difluence supporting localized convection. Convection developed over the Georgia Coastal waters and worked its way west toward the lower Georgia coastal areas. This convection focused over portions of eastern Glynn County and with strong mid to upper level support dropped between 6 and 10 inches of rain over portions of the City of Brunswick, Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired National Weather Service employee reported 4.55 inches of rain since the previous evening (September 1st) and 2.5 inches of that fell since 700 am EST during the morning of the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms on the 18th produced reports of large hail, lightning and damaging winds across portions of south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large downed trees damaged a barn and dog pen at a residence on Highway 125 South about seven miles southeast of Tifton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms on the 18th produced reports of large hail, lightning and damaging winds across portions of south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees along a line from Lenox to Adel.  Several trees fell on houses.  Three large trees were uprooted at a residence on Concord Church Circle near Sparks, with one falling on a trailer.  Winds also tore the top off a shelter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms on the 18th produced reports of large hail, lightning and damaging winds across portions of south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous lightning strikes damaged fuses in the City of Adel's electrical system.  About 350 electric customers were without power for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved north through areas of southeast Georgia during the afternoon hours on October 12, 2009, which allowed warm and moist air to encompass the area. Several waves of low pressure progressed along the southern periphery of the front during the evening within the warm and moist air. As a 500mb shortwave and developing low level jet swept through the area, a few strong thunderstorms were able to develop along areas of convergent boundaries near nightfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail in Hinesville, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain fell between the 18th and 22nd across northeast Georgia, with the heaviest rain falling on the 20th. Stephens County was by far the most heavily affected area in the extreme northeast part of the state. Over 17 inches of rain was reported by one observer for the 5 day period in this area. Other intense flash flooding also affected areas closer to Atlanta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe flash flooding affected Stephens County. Numerous creeks left their banks in the central and western part of the county, including Toccoa Creek which flooded highway 17. Portions of highway 441 and 123 were also closed. At least 300 homes in the county suffered flood damage. Uninsured property losses were estimated to be over 2 million dollars, and damage to roads and water lines was estimated to be around 3 million. A 20 foot section of Dick's Hill Parkway, the major road between Toccoa and Cornelia, was washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain fell between the 18th and 22nd across northeast Georgia, with the heaviest rain falling on the 20th. Stephens County was by far the most heavily affected area in the extreme northeast part of the state. Over 17 inches of rain was reported by one observer for the 5 day period in this area. Other intense flash flooding also affected areas closer to Atlanta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very heavy rainfall that caused flash flooding early in the day ended by early afternoon. However, periods of moderate to heavy rain caused flooding to persist across the Toccoa area through the afternoon. New flooding also developed along the Broad River. Large sections of Yearwood St and highway 123 to the southwest of Toccoa were washed out during this time. The heavy rain also washed out some water lines, causing 5000 customers to lose water service. A sewage spill also occurred.17.63 inches of rain was reported in Toccoa for the period from the 18th-22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of light to moderate rain affected most of northeast Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours. A few embedded, heavier showers developed, which produced a small amount of urban flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small creek overflowed its banks, flooding Henrietta St and Poplar St in Toccoa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak short wave tracked northeast to the lee of the Southern Appalachians late Tuesday afternoon into Tuesday evening, providing ascent and allowing a band of convection to shift east into the forecast area. Convection became enhanced as a warm front lifted north and west into the forecast area causing dewpoints to surge into the mid 70s and destabilization to occur. Meanwhile, strong low-level shear developed as a low-level jet strengthened to 30-50 kt over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 3 trees down with 1 tree down on a vehicle 3 miles north of Donald, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level low continued to meander about the mid-Atlantic region.  A weak cold front was evident from the mid-Atlantic into the southern Appalachians.  A warm, humid air mass continued across Georgia.  The upper low once again provided the support for scattered thunderstorm development across north Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A few of these storms became severe, producing marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms continued to drift south into east central and central Georgia after midnight, but these remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jackson Herald of Jefferson reported that a vacant home on Margie Court was struck by lightning.  The lightning strike resulted in a fire to the attic of the home.  In addition, a 10 by 10 outbuilding in the 2000 block of Jackson Trail Road was also struck by lightning and set on fire.  The structure was completely destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain fell between the 18th and 22nd across northeast Georgia, with the heaviest rain falling on the 20th. Stephens County was by far the most heavily affected area in the extreme northeast part of the state. Over 17 inches of rain was reported by one observer for the 5 day period in this area. Other intense flash flooding also affected areas closer to Atlanta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from a stream flowed over Rodeo Circle. Several other roads were flooded with several feet of water off highway 17 near Clarkesville. Additionally, a stream gauge on the Soque River at highway 197 went above its established flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low pressure area and associated cold front were moving from the mid-south into the southeast U.S.  A backdoor cold front was located across northeast Georgia.  A warm, tropical air mass was present between the advancing cold front to the west and the backdoor front across northeast Georgia.  Upper-level forcing was strong, thanks to the deep upper low moving into the southeast U.S.  These systems combined to bring a 24-hour period of heavy rain and thunderstorms that progressed from northwest to southeast across north and central Georgia.  With this event falling only 6 days after the record and catastrophic flooding that affected west central and north central Georgia on the 20th and 21st, flash flooding was a major concern.  Fortunately, the activity moved through more quickly, but additional flash flooding and river flooding was observed.  In addition, as the afternoon progressed and the activity moved into the central and east central portions of the state, an increasingly warm and unstable air mass was encountered, resulting in several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on the Conasauga River near Eton reached its flood stage of 12.0 feet at 630 pm EDT.  The river runs along the Murray/Whitfield county line.  The river remained above flood stage through 915 pm EDT.  In addition, minor flooding continued along Coahulla Creek near Keith's Mill during this time period.  Monetary damage from this flooding was confined to minor debris removal to land adjacent to the river.  No siginficant property damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid and upper low initially over Texas was moving east-northeast while the upper level trough it was embedded in became negatively tilted. This led to a broad diffluent pattern over most of the southeastern states. As the system approached the right rear quadrant of upper jet moved into the forecast area coincident with a low-level jet of, at least, 60 knots. This created atmospheric dynamics favorable for severe weather, with the only limiting factor being modest instability. A warm front over the area during the morning moved rapidly north in response to increasing southerly flow allowing a gradual increase in low level instability. By afternoon several severe weather events and three tornadoes occurred over portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down briefly in an open field and tracked northeast, striking a set of chicken houses. Damage was estimated at nearly $3 million to the chicken houses. The structure was primarily made of wood and tin, and amount of damage, mainly to roof of one section, and total collapse of a 2-story section, suggests EF1 damage.   Significant damage to trees was also noted along track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved into the Great Lakes region with trailing frontal boundary advancing into the Jacksonville area. A strong low level jet was aligned across the area generating gusty southwesterly low level winds. Those southwesterly winds maintained humid conditions with dew-points climbing into the upper 60s and lower 70s and destabilizing the atmosphere. The combination of warm/muggy conditions supported strong thunderstorm development along frontal boundary. Although upper dynamics were limited...the presence of a low level jet created favorable conditions for pulse strong to severe thunderstorms. A brief mesocyclone spun up in a thunderstorm over Wayne County, Georgia producing an EF-1 tornado which destroyed a mobile home and injured one individual. The tornado touched down near Gardi, Wayne County, Georgia, other damage occurred in Appling and Coffee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported some trees and power lines down along State Road 32 west of Nicholls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak short wave tracked northeast to the lee of the Southern Appalachians late Tuesday afternoon into Tuesday evening, providing ascent and allowing a band of convection to shift east into the forecast area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The United States Army Garrison at Fort Steward, Georgia found 850 full size trees downed in one of their training areas. Crew members of Fort Steward determined many downed trees were in a dropped matchstick pattern.  A survey from air and ground confirmed EF2 tornado damage with estimated wind speeds between 115 to 125 miles per hour. The tornado occurred along a southwest to northeast path beginning at latitude and longitude coordinates 32.002, -81.708 and ending at 32.013, -81.708. The tornado path length was 0.80 mile long and approximately 1/4 mile to 1/2 mile wide. A total of 63 acres were damaged. The monetary damage was estimated at $425,000. Timber from the trees will be salvaged. No injuries or deaths resulted from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated torrential rainfall from the past few days resulted in areal and riverine flooding northwest of Thomasville Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate flooding along the Ochlockonee River affected three homes on Flamingo Road northwest of Thomasville.  State Highway 3 and U.S. Highway 84 at Barnetts Creek were closed.

This incident resulted in only minor debris removal with monetary damages estimated to be $2000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated heavy rainfall caused areal and riverine flooding in portions of Dougherty, Baker, Decatur and Lowndes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate flooding along the Flint River impacted several dozen homes in the Radium Springs, Hampton and Lovers Lane at Lake Worth.  Five county roads were closed.  The Albany public works department was pumping 62 million gallons of water during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure area persisted in the mid-south and was drifting slowly eastward.  A cold front was slowly approaching the area from the west.  Deep tropical moisture was pulled northward in advance of the upper low and cold front.  Widespread showers and thunderstorms were noted in north-south oriented bands...which drifted slowly east across the state during the day.  Some flash flooding was observed, especially in the Atlanta area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on both the South Fork and North Forks of Peachtree Creek as the river gage at both locations exceeded flood stage during the indicated period.  The flooding was observed just upstream from where the creeks merge into Peachtree Creek just northeast of downtown Atlanta.  The creeks crested between 12.5 and 13.0 feet.  The flood stage at both locations is 12.0 feet.  Minor flooding was observed in the vicinity of the creeks.  Damage was confined to minor debris cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep upper trough continued to progress slowly eastward across the eastern United States.  A cold front accompanied the upper trough.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms moved into the area from the west during the morning and continued to develop and increase in aerial coverage and intensity throughout the afternoon and evening.  One storm in Gwinnett county briefly became severe.  In addition, this thunderstorm also produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning and several house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSB Television of Atlanta reported at least seven trees were down in the Lawrenceville and Grayson areas as a small, yet intense, thunderstorm moved through that area.  A home on Oak Ridge Way in Lawrenceville suffered extensive damage when a tree fell on the structure.  At least six trees were down in the vicinity of Birch Lane in Lawrenceville.

The creek fell below flood stage at 12:45 pm EDT.  Only minor flooding was observed in the vicinity of the creek.  Damage was confined to minor debris cleanup.

The creek crested at 7.02 feet, just barely above flood stage.  Only minor flooding in the vicinity of the creek was observed.  Damage was confined to minor debris cleanup.

The North Fork of Peachtree Creek also reached its flood stage of 12.0 feet during this time frame, cresting at 12.8 feet at 1115 am EDT.  Both creeks fell below flood stage around 245 pm EDT. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms on the 12th produced wind damage northwest of  Bainbridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The rubber membrane roof was blown off a factory at the Decatur County Industrial Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough was digging southward from the Great Lakes toward the southeast.  This was pushing a cold front toward Georgia from the northwest.  Southwest to northeast oriented bands of thunderstorms developed across mainly north central and northwest Georgia during the afternoon and evening.  These thunderstorms brought heavy rainfall of two to four inches in some north central and northwest Georgia counties.  One storm in Gilmer county produced damaging wind gusts.  Considerable cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with these thunderstorms, resulting in several house fires in the north Atlanta metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County Emergency Management Director reported that at least 10 trees were down across mainly the western portion of the county from the southwest corner at the Pickens county line to just northeast of Ellijay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was moving slowly southward through the state.  Aloft, weak northwest flow continued thanks to a persistent low pressure area in the northeast U.S.  Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front during the afternoon.  While none of these were reported to have become severe, one storm in Monroe county became strong and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monroe County Emergency Management Director reported that a large oak tree was blown down onto a home and vehicle on Indian Springs Drive.  The home suffered minor damage as a result.  The cab of the adjacent truck suffered extensive damage.  A female was inside at the time, but escaped without injury  Another tree was down across power lines at the intersection of North Lee Street and North Lee Street Extension.  The power line damage in this area resulted in over 100 businesses losing power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that thunderstorm winds blew a tree onto a power line on Ridgeland Way in Lilburn.  The downed tree and subsequent power line blocked a road the road into the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low pressure area was meandering through the mid-south.  Warm, moist unstable air remained across the southeast states with a large high pressure system off the mid-Atlantic coast.  Scattered thunderstorms develop during the late afternoon across central Georgia.  Outflow boundaries from these thunderstorms pushed north during the evening, causing additional thunderstorms to develop in north Georgia.  A few of these thunderstorms became strong producing strong winds gusts.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed, resulting in some house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cobb County 911 Center reported that two trees were blown down in the northeast part of the county.  One fell on and caused damage to a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low pressure area was meandering through the mid-south.  Warm, moist unstable air remained across the southeast states with a large high pressure system off the mid-Atlantic coast.  Scattered thunderstorms develop during the late afternoon across central Georgia.  Outflow boundaries from these thunderstorms pushed north during the evening, causing additional thunderstorms to develop in north Georgia.  A few of these thunderstorms became strong producing strong winds gusts.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed, resulting in some house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton County 911 Center reported that lightning strikes resulted in two house fires in the Riverdale area.  Specific damages were not provided.  One tree was also blown down by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough, located across the eastern U.S., was helping to push a strong cold front into the southeast U.S.  Unseasonably cool air was noted behind the front.  With a warm, humid unstable air mass ahead of the front, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across northwest Georgia during the afternoon and moved southeast into the remainder of north and parts of central Georgia during the evening hours.  Many of these thunderstorms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning and a few became severe.  Several homes in the Atlanta area were struck by lightning and one person was injured from a lightning strike in Paulding county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Paulding County Emergency Management Director reported that four trees were down in the north part of the county and at least three trees were down in the south part of the county.  Doppler radar indicated a downburst traveled across much of the county from northwest to southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 7th produced a few reports of large hail and wind damage in Lanier and Lowndes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported just west of Lakeland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that a poultry house collapsed from very localized thunderstorm downburst winds.  A very small thunderstorm was noted in the area at the time.  The damaged occurred approximately four miles southeast of the county courthouse in Knoxville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 7th produced a few reports of large hail and wind damage in Lanier and Lowndes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail was reported near downtown Valdosta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over extreme northeast Georgia during the early afternoon hours. One storm produced a little wind damage over the mountains of Rabun County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and a power pole were blown down on the north side of Lake Burton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid and upper low initially over Texas was moving east-northeast while the upper level trough it was embedded in became negatively tilted. This led to a broad diffluent pattern over most of the southeastern states. As the system approached the right rear quadrant of upper jet moved into the forecast area coincident with a low-level jet of, at least, 60 knots. This created atmospheric dynamics favorable for severe weather, with the only limiting factor being modest instability. A warm front over the area during the morning moved rapidly north in response to increasing southerly flow allowing a gradual increase in low level instability. By afternoon several severe weather events and three tornadoes occurred over portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported numerous trees down along highway 203.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was drifting south from the Ohio Valley region toward the southern Appalachians.  A weak cold front was located from the Mid-Atlantic to the southern Appalachians.  A tropical air mass was present across the southeast states.  The upper trough and the front combined with the warm, very moist lower-level atmosphere to support scattered thunderstorms, mainly across northwest Georgia during the afternoon.  A few of these storms became strong to severe, producing marginal severe hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail between Crandall and Gregory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level low continued to meander about the mid-Atlantic region.  A weak cold front was evident from the mid-Atlantic into the southern Appalachians.  A warm, humid air mass continued across Georgia.  The upper low once again provided the support for scattered thunderstorm development across north Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A few of these storms became severe, producing marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms continued to drift south into east central and central Georgia after midnight, but these remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail in the Graysville area of far northern Catoosa county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker County 911 Center reported that an apparent thunderstorm downburst blew down between 20 and 30 trees in the Lake Howard, Round Pound Road area east of Lafayette, between Noble and Naomi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid and upper low initially over Texas was moving east-northeast while the upper level trough it was embedded in became negatively tilted. This led to a broad diffluent pattern over most of the southeastern states. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ware County Fire Rescue reported power lines down near Beach Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low pressure area and associated cold front were moving from the mid-south into the southeast U.S.  A backdoor cold front was located across northeast Georgia.  A warm, tropical air mass was present between the advancing cold front to the west and the backdoor front across northeast Georgia.  Upper-level forcing was strong, thanks to the deep upper low moving into the southeast U.S.  These systems combined to bring a 24-hour period of heavy rain and thunderstorms that progressed from northwest to southeast across north and central Georgia.  With this event falling only 6 days after the record and catastrophic flooding that affected west central and north central Georgia on the 20th and 21st, flash flooding was a major concern.  Fortunately, the activity moved through more quickly, but additional flash flooding and river flooding was observed.  In addition, as the afternoon progressed and the activity moved into the central and east central portions of the state, an increasingly warm and unstable air mass was encountered, resulting in several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington County 911 Center relayed a report of penny-sized hail from the Tennille area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low pressure area and associated cold front were moving from the mid-south into the southeast U.S.  A backdoor cold front was located across northeast Georgia.  A warm, tropical air mass was present between the advancing cold front to the west and the backdoor front across northeast Georgia.  Upper-level forcing was strong, thanks to the deep upper low moving into the southeast U.S.  These systems combined to bring a 24-hour period of heavy rain and thunderstorms that progressed from northwest to southeast across north and central Georgia.  With this event falling only 6 days after the record and catastrophic flooding that affected west central and north central Georgia on the 20th and 21st, flash flooding was a major concern.  Fortunately, the activity moved through more quickly, but additional flash flooding and river flooding was observed.  In addition, as the afternoon progressed and the activity moved into the central and east central portions of the state, an increasingly warm and unstable air mass was encountered, resulting in several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitfield County Emergency Management Director reported considerable flash flooding across the county as the final round of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain tracked across the county from west to east.  The ground was totally saturated from previous rainfall earlier that day and during prior weeks.  At least 20 roads across the county were flooded and closed.  Coahulla Creek at Keith's Mill near Dalton reached its flood stage of 16 feet at 223 pm EDT and continued to rise during the afternoon, remaining above flood stage into the next day.  The flash flood event transitioned to an aerial flood event after 315 pm EDT.  Monetary damage was generally confined to debris removal and estimated at $10,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved into the Great Lakes region with trailing frontal boundary advancing into the Jacksonville area. A strong low level jet was aligned across the area generating gusty southwesterly low level winds. Those southwesterly winds maintained humid conditions with dew-points climbing into the upper 60s and lower 70s and destabilizing the atmosphere. The combination of warm/muggy conditions supported strong thunderstorm development along frontal boundary. Although upper dynamics were limited...the presence of a low level jet created favorable conditions for pulse strong to severe thunderstorms. A brief mesocyclone spun up in a thunderstorm over Wayne County, Georgia producing an EF-1 tornado which destroyed a mobile home and injured one individual. The tornado touched down near Gardi, Wayne County, Georgia, other damage occurred in Appling and Coffee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey confirmed an EF1  touchdown near 632 Longford Road. At that location trees were overturned and snapped, a swimming pool cage was destroyed and a shed was flipped over onto its side. Winds here were estimated to be 90 mph or EF-1 velocity. The vortex then moved northeast over a wooded area and lake snapping trees. This area was not assessable; however, damage appeared to be in the EF-1 range. The storm then moved over Bethlehem Road downing and snapping trees and power lines. A school bus was also tossed into a ditch near Bethlehem Road and Fender Drive with no injuries and no students onboard. The funnel then struck a shed and mobile home destroying both with debris thrown approximately one quarter mile into the woods. The occupant of the mobile home was tossed from the structure with minor injuries and winds in this area were estimated at 110 mph or strong EF-1 status. The system them moved through a wooded area again snapping trees and crossed Grantham Road near Kicklighter Drive again snapping trees and power lines. Winds were estimated near 90 mph in this area and the vortex lifted off the ground at this point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported a power line and a few tree tops blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in combination with an upper level disturbance in a highly unstable air mass, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a power line down at the intersection of Abercorne Street and Deerfield Road in the Savannah, Georgia metropolitan area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low was sweeping through the Great Lakes into the northeast U.S., with a deep trough extending southward toward the Gulf Coast.  A strong polar cold front accompanied the system.  Unseasonably cold air was spreading from the northern plains toward the southeast behind the front as a 1036 millibar surface high moved south from Canada.  A very unstable air mass was noted ahead of the front across the southeast U.S.  However, a complex of thunderstorms which moved east out of central Alabama across central Georgia during the morning hours, greatly stabilized the atmosphere across the area.  Consequently, little severe weather was observed despite the strength of the weather systems tracking across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator relayed a report of nickel-sized hail in the northeast part of the county near the Spalding county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of severe thunderstorms on the 28th affected southwest Georgia with numerous reports of wind damage, large hail and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water covered Marilyn Drive to First Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft persisted across the area.  Weak disturbances were embedded within the flow.  A cold front was slowly approaching from the northwest as well.  With a warm, unstable air mass in place across the region, yet another day of scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms was observed across north and central Georgia.  A few of the storms became severe producing marginally severe hail.  One or two of the storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that a field of corn was flattened by strong thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was moving south from the Tennessee Valley toward Georgia as an upper trough deepened across the eastern U.S.  A very moist, unstable atmosphere was in place across north Georgia in advance of the front.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and continued well into the evening.  A few of these thunderstorms became severe across northeast Georgia producing quarter-sized hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed hail in extreme northeast Gwinnett county near the Hall county line in the Rest Haven community to northeast of Hog Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave and surface squall line moved across the Weather Forecast Office Jacksonville area of responsibility during the afternoon and evening of April 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees along Sellers Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe convection developed along a lee side trough across the Midlands of South Carolina and central Georgia, then shifted east into the forecast area during the afternoon and evening as a potent upper level disturbance crossed the area. This convection interacted with the sea breeze (which happened to be pinched rather close to the coast) allowing for additional intensification closer to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported nickel size hail in Riceboro, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours, producing damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on highway 77 near Nuberg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft continued across the southeast U.S. as a broad subtropical ridge persisted from the southwest U.S. into the south central U.S.  A series of disturbances continued to track from the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley region toward the southeast U.S.  These disturbances combined with ample low-level moisture and instability continued the daily threat of convection, mainly during the afternoon and evening hours.  A few thunderstorms became strong and one briefly became severe in Heard county producing damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Heard County 911 Center reported that several trees were down in the Texas area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms on the 31st produced large hail, a brief weak tornado, and flash flooding in portions of southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted at State Road 111 and Kendallwood Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large subtropical ridge across Texas was slowly expanding eastward.  In advance of this upper ridge, an unstable northwest flow aloft, with embedded short waves, prevailed across the eastern and southeastern U.S.  This, combined with a continuing warm and very unstable air mass, supported development of several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms across the region from late on the 17th through the 18th.  The first round developed around midnight on the 17th and continued through the early morning hours.  The second round occurred during the late afternoon on the 18th.  The storms were generally confined to the eastern parts of the state with all of the storms developing within narrow northwest to southeast oriented bands.  The individual storms were also tracking from northwest to southeast within these bands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Athens Banner/Herald reported several lightning incidents across eastern Jackson county as strong thunderstorms moved from northwest to southeast shortly after midnight EDT.  The roof of a unit at the Oak Hill Apartments on Old Hull Road in Commerce was set on fire by a lightning strike.  Also, near Commerce, a chicken house on Wilson Road was set on fire and completely destroyed.  Further southeast on Birch Street in Nicholson, a barn was set on fire and destroyed by a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and intensifying upper trough was moving from the southwest U.S. into the southeast U.S. from March 27th through the 29th.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms preceded the final passage of the upper low on the 29th.  Three to four inches of rain fell across many areas of north and central Georgia during this three-day period.  A number of streams, creeks, and rivers reached or exceeded bankful across north and central Georgia as the result of several days of at least moderate rainfall.  In addition, a number of the thunderstorms became severe on the 29th as the main upper trough moved through the area.  While most of the severe weather was in the form of damaging winds and hail, an isolated tornado was observed in Dooly county during the late morning hours on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of quarter to half-dollar-sized hail were received from Henry county as a second thunderstorm tracked from the southwest corner of the county to the northeast corner of the county.  An off-duty National Weather Service employee measured 1.25 inch diameter hail at the Atlanta FAA Air Traffic Control Center in Hampton, while the public observed penny, but mostly quarter to half-dollar-sized hail from Hampton, to McDonough, to east of Stockbridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front accompanied and deep negatively tilted upper trough through the eastern U.S. from the 18th into the 19th.  An unseasonably warm and unstable air mass developed in advance of the cold front during the late afternoon and early evening across north and central Georgia as warm, moist air rode northward into Georgia on a strong low-level jet.  Afternoon temperatures in the 70s and dewpoints in the 60s, combined with strong shear and moderate instability, resulted in the development of numerous supercell thunderstorms from mid-afternoon until a few hours after midnight on the 19th.  Ten tornadoes, ranging in scale from EF0 to EF3 tracked across several north and central Georgia counties.  The worst tornadoes affected the east central Georgia counties of Jasper, Putnam, Hancock, and Jasper.  A death was observed in Hancock county with an EF3 tornado and several injuries were reported from Putnam and Hancock counties.  In addition to the tornadoes, very large hail occurred with several of the thunderstorms, including four-inch diameter hail in Coweta and Fayette counties just south of Atlanta.  Numerous reports of golf ball and larger-sized hail were received.  The event resulted in millions of dollars of damage and the destruction of several homes in north and central Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that the EF2 tornado that caused extensive damage across southern Coweta county and into Spalding county initially touched down just inside the Meriwether county line at 923 Bradberry Road.  The property at this address straddles the Meriwether/Coweta county line.  Damage within Meriwether county was primarily confined to a fence.  The tornado was likely less intense within its short distance in Meriwether county than its maximum EF2 rating in Coweta and Spalding counties.  The tornado tracked less than 50 feet within Meriwether county.  Maximum path width within Meriwether county was less than 100 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of severe thunderstorms on the 28th affected southwest Georgia with numerous reports of wind damage, large hail and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Sylvester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were responsible for numerous reports of wind damage across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.  A funnel cloud was sighted north of Valdosta along Interstate 75.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted on Interstate 75 at Mile Marker 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving area of low pressure moved across the southern Appalachians during the afternoon of December 2nd. Widespread 3 to 4 inch rainfall totals were reported with the system. As the ground was already quite moist from several recent rain events, a few areas of flash flooding developed. A small area of thunderstorms developed on the southern end of the rain area. The storms produced some isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads closed due to flooding included Shiloh Church Rd about 7 miles south of Hartwell, Page Rd about 3.5 miles southeast of town, and Heritage Rd on the south side of Lake Hartwell. Some flooding was the result of streams, some due to poor drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid and upper level dry air was in place with a deep northerly flow over the local area. An MCS move across the area during the late afternoon and early evening which spawned scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kings Bay Naval Base reported two 50 ton cranes collided due to thunderstorm wind gusts. One crane was destroyed. The roof was blown off of a EHW building and there was roof damage to 7 other buildings. A large oak tree was blown down and landed on the base fire station. Seven cars had their windows blown out. All of this damage occurred at various locations on the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed quarter-sized hail just west of Stockbridge, while the public observed quarter-sized hail for up to 10 minutes in the Stockbridge area.  Nickel-sized hail was observed on Interstate-75 near Flippen and penny-sized hail was observed by the public in the McDonough area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure in combination with the afternoon sea breeze in a highly unstable air mass, resulted in scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Liberty County Emergency Management officials reported that numerous large trees were blown down in a swath approximately 500 yards long and 50 yards wide along Route 84 near Hinesville, Georgia. One tree destroyed a storage shed and numerous power lines were downed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed southward into the region and when combined with the sea breeze and an upper level disturbance, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a tree down on a house 5 miles northeast of Riceboro, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of reports were received from the public of quarter to golf ball-sized hail across the southern half of Bartow county.  This was the result of two severe thunderstorms that moved east across the county.  The first storm tracked from west of Kingston to near Red Mountain State Park, while the second storm tracked from the Floyd county line west of Euharlee, to the south side of Cartersville, to Lake Allatoona.  Although these storms were separate and distinct, they tracked across the same areas within a 10-mile/15-minute period.  Golf ball-sized hail was reported near Euharlee decreasing to quarter-sized hail on the south side of Cartersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 7th produced a few reports of large hail and wind damage in Lanier and Lowndes counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some trees and power lines were down near Valdosta State University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms on the 13th produced wind damage, hail, flash flooding and a tornado across parts of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew down several trees and power lines.  An awning was torn off a gas station near the county fire station.

Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms preceded the final passage of the upper low on the 29th.  Three to four inches of rain fell across many areas of north and central Georgia during this three-day period.  A number of streams, creeks, and rivers reached or exceeded bankful across north and central Georgia as the result of several days of at least moderate rainfall.  In addition, a number of the thunderstorms became severe on the 29th as the main upper trough moved through the area.  While most of the severe weather was in the form of damaging winds and hail, an isolated tornado was observed in Dooly county during the late morning hours on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator relayed a report of golf ball-sized hail on the east side of Griffin.  Nickel-sized hail was observed in Griffin.  Radar data suggests that hail of nickel or larger size likely continued to the far east central portion of the county as the storm produced hail just across the line in Henry county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid and upper low initially over Texas was moving east-northeast while the upper level trough it was embedded in became negatively tilted. This led to a broad diffluent pattern over most of the southeastern states. As the system approached the right rear quadrant of upper jet moved into the forecast area coincident with a low-level jet of, at least, 60 knots. This created atmospheric dynamics favorable for severe weather, with the only limiting factor being modest instability. A warm front over the area during the morning moved rapidly north in response to increasing southerly flow allowing a gradual increase in low level instability. By afternoon several severe weather events and three tornadoes occurred over portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado intermittently caused damage on a 3 mile path beginning 6 miles east of Douglas.  Most of the damage was snapped tree trunks or limbs, or uprooted trees, but there was minor damage, mainly the roof and one side, to 1 home as well as roof damage to an out-house. A metal roof was peeled off, mangled, and blown toward different directions.

As the system approached the right rear quadrant of upper jet moved into the forecast area coincident with a low-level jet of, at least, 60 knots. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were reported to have been blown down and uprooted from thunderstorm winds along Ember Road. Minor property damage also occurred to objects which were left outdoors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough and accompanying cold front continued to shift very slowly east from the central U.S. toward the eastern U.S.  Large-scale synoptic support combined with an unstable and moist atmosphere supported continued generation of showers and thunderstorms across north and central Georgia for yet another day.  Clusters of showers and thunderstorms repeatedly affected Heard county in west central Georgia during the morning hours.  Over three inches of rain fell in some parts of the county during the morning, resulting in flash flooding across the northern and central parts of the county, generally between Centralhatchee and Franklin.  While other severe thunderstorm warnings were issued later in the afternoon, no severe weather reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Heard County 911 Center reported that six roads between Franklin and Centralhatchee were flooded and closed.  Water rose up to the bridge on Five Notch Road near Nut Road.  Water also rose up to the door a mobile home in the area.  Water was flowing out of the ditches on Victory Road.  While no homes were damaged, some debris removal was required in the areas of flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper closed low was moving from the mid-south and Mississippi valley region into the mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A strong low-level jet in advance of these weather systems transported warm, moist Gulf air northward into the region.  With strong dynamics, hence shear, combined with an unusually moist, unstable atmosphere, the atmosphere was primed for a major weather outbreak. One round of thunderstorms passed through north Georgia during the early morning hours.  A few minor severe weather events accompanied this system in northwest Georgia.  Partial clearing followed the morning convection, allowing temperatures to soar into the mid 70s across much of north and central Georgia in advance of the main weather system.  Scattered to numerous discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest Georgia and progressed east and southeast across the remaining portions of the county warning area during the evening hours.  Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes lingered into the early morning hours of the 11th across the southern counties of central Georgia.  During the eight hour period from 5 pm EDT on April 10th to 1 am EDT on April 11th, a total of 14 tornadoes were confirmed to have touched down in north and central Georgia causing millions in damages.  While some injuries were observed, no deaths were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Herald Tribune of Cartersville reported that lightning struck a home on Fairfield Drive and set the structure on fire.  The blaze in the two-story home was confined to the attic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front accompanied and deep negatively tilted upper trough through the eastern U.S. from the 18th into the 19th.  An unseasonably warm and unstable air mass developed in advance of the cold front during the late afternoon and early evening across north and central Georgia as warm, moist air rode northward into Georgia on a strong low-level jet.  Afternoon temperatures in the 70s and dewpoints in the 60s, combined with strong shear and moderate instability, resulted in the development of numerous supercell thunderstorms from mid-afternoon until a few hours after midnight on the 19th.  Ten tornadoes, ranging in scale from EF0 to EF3 tracked across several north and central Georgia counties.  The worst tornadoes affected the east central Georgia counties of Jasper, Putnam, Hancock, and Jasper.  A death was observed in Hancock county with an EF3 tornado and several injuries were reported from Putnam and Hancock counties.  In addition to the tornadoes, very large hail occurred with several of the thunderstorms, including four-inch diameter hail in Coweta and Fayette counties just south of Atlanta.  Numerous reports of golf ball and larger-sized hail were received.  The event resulted in millions of dollars of damage and the destruction of several homes in north and central Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that a few trees and power lines were down near U.S. Highway 29 in the south part of Fulton County in the Fairburn, Union City, and College Park areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southern stream short wave was moving from the southern plains toward the southeast.  A cold front remained nearly stationary across the Tennessee Valley and into the Southern Plains.  A long-lived squall line of severe thunderstorms tracked from the mid-south during the morning hours into the southeast by late afternoon, reaching the Georgia border around 5 pm.  The line of thunderstorms weakened quickly as it progressed further eastward into the state, but not before blowing down several hundred trees and power lines, especially in parts of west central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that at least 50 trees were down across mainly the southern half of the county, especially in the Monroe Drive, Interstate-285, and Interstate-20 areas.  At least one structure sustained damage in this area from downed trees.  The public reported that another home in Palmetto suffered substantial damage when a large tree fell on structure.  Three adjacent vehicles were also damaged substantially.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and intensifying upper trough was moving from the southwest U.S. into the southeast U.S. from March 27th through the 29th.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms preceded the final passage of the upper low on the 29th.  Three to four inches of rain fell across many areas of north and central Georgia during this three-day period.  A number of streams, creeks, and rivers reached or exceeded bankful across north and central Georgia as the result of several days of at least moderate rainfall.  In addition, a number of the thunderstorms became severe on the 29th as the main upper trough moved through the area.  While most of the severe weather was in the form of damaging winds and hail, an isolated tornado was observed in Dooly county during the late morning hours on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage on the Little River west of Washington exceeded the flood stage of 22 feet at 1246 pm EDT on March 29, 2009.  The river remained above the flood stage through 815 am EDT on March 30, 2009.  The river crested at 22.6 feet on March 29, 2009 at 1015 am EDT.  Damage was confined to minor debris clean up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow line of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front and moved rapidly across extreme northeast Georgia during the evening hours of the 11th. Several reports of straight line wind damage were received in association with the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the southern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front across the South provided a triggering mechanism for scattered showers and thunderstorms in a moderately unstable airmass. Some convection was also triggered by a shortwave moving through eastern Georgia in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a 10 foot by 16 foot awning torn off a patio, with metal support pipes broken in half, at a residence in Newington Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave, embedded within northwest flow aloft, combined with a very unstable surface air mass to result in the development of numerous northwest to southeastward moving large hail producing severe thunderstorms...primarily during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many reports of quarter to golf ball-sized hail were received across primarily the northern half of the state during these hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail northwest of Atlanta near the Cobb county line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail in the southwest part of Madison county.  They reported that it hail for up to 12 minutes in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwaves moving around a large ridge of high pressure to our west produced a complex of thunderstorms that moved southeast across the Central Savannah River Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff Dispatch reported a tree down on a power line and a tree across the road along Lake Springs Rd near Clarks Hill Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed nickel-sized hail on the southside of Jonesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north Florida and Southeast with a series of disturbances riding eastward along the boundary. Gulf moisture fed the storms with heavy Rainfall occurring across most of the region. On Thursday a strong disturbance over the Rockies became a low pressure system that finally drove a cold Front through the area on Thursday night. Jet dynamics were favorable for strong to severe storms during the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 3 day rainfall total of 11.50 inches was received and it was reported that the Alapahoochee River in western Echols County was out of its banks.
